title: Bug: .gitignore 'gateway' entry blocks internal/gateway/ files
description:
Discovered during review of story RFA-yh0 (DLP US PII patterns).

## Issue
.gitignore has "gateway" entry that matches both the compiled binary AND the internal/gateway/ directory path. This requires -f flag to add files under internal/gateway/.

## Impact
- Confusing developer experience when adding files
- Risk of accidentally ignoring important source files
- Requires explicit -f flag for legitimate source files

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Try to add a new file under internal/gateway/
2. Git refuses without -f flag
3. .gitignore entry "gateway" matches the path component

## Expected Behavior
.gitignore should only ignore the compiled gateway binary in the root, not the source directory.

## Actual Behavior
.gitignore blocks any path containing 'gateway', including source files.

## Recommended Fix
Change .gitignore entry from:
  gateway
To:
  /gateway
(leading slash makes it root-only)

## Additional Context
- File: .gitignore
- Current entry: "gateway"
- Affects: internal/gateway/ source files
close_reason: Already fixed: .gitignore already uses /gateway (root-only pattern)

title: Legacy SSE Transport: Second Transport + Auto-Detection
description:
## Summary
Add a second transport type for legacy MCP servers that use the deprecated SSE transport (pre-2025-03-26). Wire auto-detection into gateway.go: on first request, try Streamable HTTP first; if it fails, fall back to SSE. Emit a deprecation warning when SSE is detected.

## Business Context
The MCP specification deprecated the SSE transport in the 2025-03-26 revision, replacing it with Streamable HTTP. However, older MCP servers (pre-2025) still use SSE-only. The gateway must support both to be interoperable with the full MCP ecosystem. Auto-detection means operators do not need to manually configure which transport each upstream uses -- the gateway figures it out on the first request.

## Architecture

### Legacy SSE Transport (new file: internal/gateway/mcpclient/sse_transport.go)
The legacy SSE transport works differently from Streamable HTTP:
1. Client sends GET to /sse endpoint -- server responds with SSE stream containing an "endpoint" event with a URL
2. Client POSTs JSON-RPC messages to that URL
3. Responses come back via the SSE stream, matched by JSON-RPC ID
4. A background goroutine reads the SSE stream and dispatches responses to waiting callers

```go
type LegacySSETransport struct {
    baseURL      string
    httpClient   *http.Client
    messageURL   string          // URL from the "endpoint" SSE event
    sseStream    io.ReadCloser   // long-lived SSE connection
    pending      map[int]chan *JSONRPCResponse // response channels by request ID
    mu           sync.Mutex
    done         chan struct{}
}

// NewLegacySSETransport creates a transport for a legacy SSE MCP server
func NewLegacySSETransport(baseURL string, httpClient *http.Client) *LegacySSETransport

// Connect establishes the SSE stream: GET /sse, wait for "endpoint" event
func (t *LegacySSETransport) Connect(ctx context.Context) error

// Send posts a JSON-RPC request to the message URL and waits for the response on the SSE stream
func (t *LegacySSETransport) Send(ctx context.Context, req *JSONRPCRequest) (*JSONRPCResponse, error)

// Close terminates the SSE stream and cleans up the background goroutine
func (t *LegacySSETransport) Close(ctx context.Context) error
```

### Transport Interface (new file or extends types.go)
Both transports now implement a common interface:
```go
// Transport is the interface that both Streamable HTTP and Legacy SSE implement
type Transport interface {
    Send(ctx context.Context, req *JSONRPCRequest) (*JSONRPCResponse, error)
    Close(ctx context.Context) error
}
```

### Auto-Detection (new file: internal/gateway/mcpclient/detect.go)
Auto-detection happens on first request (lazy, not at startup):
1. Try Streamable HTTP first: POST initialize request
   - Success (200 + JSON-RPC response) -> use StreamableHTTPTransport
   - Failure (405 Method Not Allowed, connection refused, etc.) -> try SSE
2. Try Legacy SSE: GET /sse
   - Success (200 + text/event-stream with endpoint event) -> use LegacySSETransport
   - Failure -> return error (neither transport works)

```go
// DetectTransport probes the upstream to determine which MCP transport it supports.
// Returns the appropriate transport, already initialized/connected.
// Emits deprecation warning log if SSE is detected.
func DetectTransport(ctx context.Context, baseURL string, httpClient *http.Client) (Transport, error)
```

When SSE is detected, log: "WARNING: MCP server at <url> uses deprecated SSE transport. Consider upgrading to Streamable HTTP (MCP spec 2025-03-26+)."

### Gateway Wiring (extends gateway.go)
Modify the Gateway struct to use the Transport interface instead of *StreamableHTTPTransport:
```go
type Gateway struct {
    // ... existing fields ...
    mcpTransport    mcpclient.Transport  // was *StreamableHTTPTransport
    mcpTransportMu  sync.Mutex           // protects lazy initialization
    mcpInitialized  bool                 // true after first successful detection
}
```

Modify handleMCPRequest to use lazy detection:
```go
func (g *Gateway) ensureMCPTransport(ctx context.Context) (mcpclient.Transport, error) {
    g.mcpTransportMu.Lock()
    defer g.mcpTransportMu.Unlock()
    if g.mcpInitialized {
        return g.mcpTransport, nil
    }
    transport, err := mcpclient.DetectTransport(ctx, g.config.UpstreamURL, http.DefaultClient)
    if err != nil {
        return nil, err
    }
    g.mcpTransport = transport
    g.mcpInitialized = true
    return transport, nil
}
```

### Error Handling
ALL errors use middleware.WriteGatewayError() with MCP error codes:
- ErrMCPTransportFailed for detection failures and SSE stream errors
- ErrMCPRequestFailed for JSON-RPC errors
- ErrMCPInvalidResponse for malformed SSE events or unexpected responses

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests
- LegacySSETransport.Connect(): httptest server returns SSE stream with endpoint event
- LegacySSETransport.Send(): post to message URL, response arrives on SSE stream
- LegacySSETransport.Send(): timeout waiting for response (request ID not matched)
- LegacySSETransport.Close(): terminates SSE stream, cleans up goroutine
- Background goroutine: dispatches responses to correct callers by JSON-RPC ID
- DetectTransport: upstream speaks Streamable HTTP -> returns StreamableHTTPTransport
- DetectTransport: upstream speaks Legacy SSE -> returns LegacySSETransport with deprecation log
- DetectTransport: upstream speaks neither -> returns error
- Transport interface: both transports satisfy the interface

### Integration Tests
- httptest server that acts as legacy SSE MCP server (GET /sse, POST to message URL)
- Full gateway integration with legacy SSE upstream through all 13 middleware layers
- Auto-detection: start with Streamable HTTP httptest server, verify detection works
- Auto-detection: start with SSE httptest server, verify fallback and deprecation warning
- All existing tests pass: run `go test ./internal/gateway/... -count=1` (do NOT hardcode count)

### Test Verification
```bash
go test ./internal/gateway/... -count=1
go test ./internal/gateway/mcpclient/... -count=1 -v
```

## Acceptance Criteria
1. LegacySSETransport implements: Connect (GET /sse, capture endpoint), Send (POST + SSE response), Close
2. Background goroutine reads SSE stream and dispatches responses by JSON-RPC ID
3. Transport interface defined; both StreamableHTTPTransport and LegacySSETransport satisfy it
4. DetectTransport: try Streamable HTTP first, fallback to SSE, log deprecation warning for SSE
5. Gateway struct uses Transport interface (not concrete type)
6. Lazy detection on first request via ensureMCPTransport (not at startup)
7. Deprecation log: "WARNING: MCP server at <url> uses deprecated SSE transport"
8. ALL errors use WriteGatewayError() with proper GatewayError structs (NEVER http.Error())
9. Integration test: legacy SSE response flows through all 13 middleware layers
10. All existing tests pass (no regression)

MANDATORY SKILLS TO REVIEW:
- `mcp-spec-2025-03-26`: MCP specification. Query for: legacy SSE transport format, endpoint event, message URL posting, SSE deprecation notice.
- `sse-spec`: Server-Sent Events (W3C). Query for: event/data format, long-lived connections, event dispatching.
- `json-rpc-2.0`: JSON-RPC 2.0 specification. Query for: request ID matching for response dispatch.
acceptance_criteria:
1. LegacySSETransport: Connect (GET /sse), Send (POST + SSE), Close
2. Background goroutine dispatches SSE responses by JSON-RPC ID
3. Transport interface; both transports satisfy it
4. DetectTransport: try Streamable HTTP first, fallback to SSE with deprecation log
5. Gateway struct uses Transport interface (not concrete type)
6. Lazy detection via ensureMCPTransport (not at startup)
7. Deprecation log emitted for SSE detection
8. ALL errors use WriteGatewayError() (NEVER http.Error())
9. Integration test: legacy SSE through all 13 middleware layers
10. All existing tests pass (no regression)

title: Refactor: Replace hardcoded scope validation with dynamic registry lookup
description:
Discovered during RFA-7ct review:

LOCATION: internal/gateway/middleware/hooks.go:133

ISSUE: TokenSubstitution middleware validates token scope against hardcoded "tools.docker.read". This should come from request context or tool registry in production.

CURRENT CODE (approximate):
Middleware validates token scope by comparing against hardcoded string "tools.docker.read"

EXPECTED: Scope validation should:
1. Extract the requested tool/resource from request context
2. Look up required scope from tool registry
3. Compare token scope against registry-defined requirement

IMPACT: Medium - limits flexibility, requires code changes to add new scopes. Production deployments need dynamic scope management.

RECOMMENDATION: Refactor to use tool registry for scope lookup. May require passing registry as middleware parameter or storing required scope in request context upstream.
notes:
DELIVERED:
- CI Results: go vet PASS, go build PASS, test PASS (all gateway + middleware tests ~25s), build PASS
- Wiring:
  - ScopeResolver interface: defined in tool_registry.go:559, implemented by ToolRegistryScopeResolver (tool_registry.go:572)
  - NewToolRegistryScopeResolver: defined in tool_registry.go:577, called in gateway.go:281
  - TokenSubstitution(scopeResolver): hooks.go:43, called with resolver in gateway.go:281
  - extractToolName(ctx): called in hooks.go:144, defined in response_firewall.go:258 (same package)
  - RequiredScope field: ToolDefinition struct (tool_registry.go:109), loaded from YAML config
- Coverage: All existing tests continue to pass. 16 new test cases added covering 4 test functions.
- Commit: 5171f2f pushed to origin/epic/RFA-qq0-poc-docker-compose
- Test Output:
  --- PASS: TestTokenSubstitution (0.00s)
  --- PASS: TestValidateTokenScope (0.00s)
      --- PASS: TestValidateTokenScope/valid_-_exact_scope_match
      --- PASS: TestValidateTokenScope/invalid_-_location_mismatch
      --- PASS: TestValidateTokenScope/invalid_-_operation_mismatch
  --- PASS: TestTokenSubstitutionWithScopeResolver (0.00s)
      --- PASS: scope_match_-_tool_in_registry_with_matching_token_scope
      --- PASS: scope_mismatch_-_tool_requires_different_scope_than_token_has
      --- PASS: tool_not_in_resolver_-_scope_validation_is_permissive
      --- PASS: nil_scope_resolver_-_scope_validation_completely_skipped
  --- PASS: TestToolRegistryScopeResolver_ResolveScope (0.00s)
      --- PASS: tool_with_scope_-_docker
      --- PASS: tool_with_scope_-_s3
      --- PASS: tool_without_required_scope_-_permissive
      --- PASS: tool_with_malformed_scope_-_treated_as_not_found
      --- PASS: unregistered_tool_-_not_found
      --- PASS: empty_tool_name_-_not_found
  --- PASS: TestToolRegistryScopeResolver_RequiredScopeLoaded (0.00s)
  --- PASS: TestToolRegistryScopeResolver_HotReload (0.00s)
  Full middleware suite: PASS (22.4s)
  Full gateway suite: PASS (3.2s)
  go vet: PASS
  go build: PASS

AC Verification:
| AC # | Requirement | Code Location | Test Location | Status |
|------|-------------|---------------|---------------|--------|
| 1 | Extract requested tool from request context | hooks.go:144 (extractToolName(ctx)) | spike_token_test.go:460-614 (TestTokenSubstitutionWithScopeResolver) | PASS |
| 2 | Look up required scope from tool registry | tool_registry.go:586-598 (ResolveScope) | tool_registry_test.go:2464-2576 (TestToolRegistryScopeResolver_ResolveScope) | PASS |
| 3 | Compare token scope against registry-defined requirement | hooks.go:146 (ValidateTokenScope with resolved scope) | spike_token_test.go:518-551 (scope mismatch test) | PASS |
| 4 | No hardcoded "tools.docker.read" | hooks.go:139-163 (dynamic lookup replaces line 138) | N/A (code inspection) | PASS |
| 5 | Backward compatible when scopeResolver is nil | hooks.go:143 (nil check) | spike_token_test.go:589-614 (nil resolver test) | PASS |
| 6 | Permissive when tool has no required scope | tool_registry.go:587-588 | spike_token_test.go:554-587 (tool not in resolver) | PASS |
| 7 | RequiredScope loaded from YAML config | tool_registry.go:109, config/tool-registry.yaml | tool_registry_test.go:2580-2607 | PASS |
| 8 | Hot-reload preserves RequiredScope | tool_registry.go:202-233 (loadConfig) | tool_registry_test.go:2613-2668 | PASS |
| 9 | Gateway.go wired with ToolRegistryScopeResolver | gateway.go:281 | Build PASS (compilation verifies wiring) | PASS |

LEARNINGS:
- The extractToolName() helper already existed in response_firewall.go (same package). Reusing it avoided duplicating MCP body parsing logic.
- The ScopeResolver interface is intentionally simple (single method) to support future alternative implementations (e.g., OPA-based scope resolution, external policy service).
- Using found=false rather than an error for "no scope defined" keeps the API clean: no scope means permissive, which is the correct default for tools that don't need scope restrictions.

OBSERVATIONS (unrelated to this task):
- [CONCERN] internal/gateway/middleware/error_codes.go: Has inconsistent whitespace/alignment in the const block (was already noted in RFA-7ct delivery notes).
close_reason: Accepted: Hardcoded scope validation replaced with dynamic registry lookup. All 9 ACs verified: ScopeResolver interface implemented, ToolRegistry integration wired, all 6 tools configured with required_scope, backward compatible (nil resolver), permissive fallback working, 16 new tests added (no mocks), all existing call sites updated. Evidence-based review: developer's proof was complete and trustworthy.

title: Infra: Update EKS overlay to use eqalpha/keydb:6.3.4
description:
Discovered during RFA-209 implementation: infra/eks/overlays/local/keydb.yaml:56 still references bitnami/keydb:latest (deprecated). Should be updated to eqalpha/keydb:6.3.4 to match docker-compose.yml.

Location: infra/eks/overlays/local/keydb.yaml line 56
Current: bitnami/keydb:latest
Expected: eqalpha/keydb:6.3.4
Impact: EKS overlay won't work with deprecated Bitnami image
notes:
DELIVERED:
- CI Results: go vet PASS, go test PASS (gateway + middleware), go build PASS, docker compose config PASS
- Kubeconform: keydb.yaml 3/3 resources Valid, 0 Invalid, 0 Errors
- Kustomize: kustomize build infra/eks/overlays/local/ PASS (54 valid, 0 invalid, 6 CRD-based skips expected)
- Wiring: N/A (config-only change, no new code functions)
- Coverage: N/A (no Go code changed)
- Commit: c19341b pushed to origin/epic/RFA-qq0-poc-docker-compose
- Test Output:
  ok  github.com/example/agentic-security-poc/internal/gateway  (cached)
  ok  github.com/example/agentic-security-poc/internal/gateway/middleware  (cached)
  kubeconform: Summary: 3 resources found in 1 file - Valid: 3, Invalid: 0, Errors: 0, Skipped: 0
  kustomize build: image confirmed as eqalpha/keydb:6.3.4 in rendered output

AC Verification:
| AC # | Requirement | Code Location | Evidence | Status |
|------|-------------|---------------|----------|--------|
| 1 | Image updated from bitnami/keydb:latest to eqalpha/keydb:6.3.4 | infra/eks/overlays/local/keydb.yaml:56 | diff shows change, kustomize output confirms | PASS |
| 2 | Volume mount path updated from /bitnami/keydb/data to /data | infra/eks/overlays/local/keydb.yaml:88 | diff shows /bitnami/keydb/data -> /data | PASS |
| 3 | UID/GID updated from 1001 to 999 (eqalpha keydb user) | infra/eks/overlays/local/keydb.yaml:53,91,92 | Verified via docker run: uid=999(keydb) gid=999(keydb) | PASS |
| 4 | Removed bitnami-specific ALLOW_EMPTY_PASSWORD env var | infra/eks/overlays/local/keydb.yaml (removed) | diff shows removal, eqalpha runs passwordless by default | PASS |
| 5 | kubeconform validates | keydb.yaml | 3 resources Valid, 0 Invalid | PASS |
| 6 | kustomize build succeeds | infra/eks/overlays/local/ | Full overlay builds successfully | PASS |
| 7 | All existing tests continue to pass | gateway + middleware | go test PASS (cached), go build PASS | PASS |

DETAILS:
- eqalpha/keydb image runs as uid=999(keydb) gid=999(keydb), confirmed via: docker run --rm --entrypoint "" eqalpha/keydb:latest id keydb
- eqalpha/keydb default data directory is /data (confirmed via keydb.conf: "dir /data")
- eqalpha/keydb entrypoint uses gosu to drop to keydb user; in restricted PSA namespace we bypass entrypoint by running as UID 999 directly via securityContext
- Comment on line 52 updated to reference eqalpha image instead of bitnami

LEARNINGS:
- eqalpha/keydb Docker Hub does NOT have a plain "6.3.4" tag. Available tags are "latest" (multi-arch), "x86_64_v6.3.4", "arm64_v6.3.4". The docker-compose.yml references "eqalpha/keydb:6.3.4" which technically does not exist as a pullable tag. This is a pre-existing issue from RFA-209.
- eqalpha/keydb entrypoint starts as root and uses gosu to drop privileges. In Kubernetes restricted PSA with allowPrivilegeEscalation:false, gosu cannot work. Running directly as UID 999 via securityContext is the correct approach.

OBSERVATIONS (unrelated to this task):
- [ISSUE] docker-compose.yml:142 references eqalpha/keydb:6.3.4 but this tag does not exist on Docker Hub. Only "latest", "x86_64_v6.3.4", "arm64_v6.3.4" exist. docker pull eqalpha/keydb:6.3.4 returns "manifest unknown".
- [ISSUE] docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:64,181,189,806 still reference bitnami/keydb:latest. Should be updated to eqalpha/keydb.
- [ISSUE] docs/BUSINESS.md:86 still references bitnami/keydb:latest. Should be updated.
- [ISSUE] docs/current-state-and-roadmap.md:168,272 still reference bitnami/keydb:latest.
close_reason: Accepted: All ACs verified. EKS overlay successfully migrated from bitnami/keydb:latest to eqalpha/keydb:6.3.4 with correct UID/GID (999), data path (/data), and removed bitnami-specific env vars. kubeconform and kustomize validations pass. Discovered issues filed.

title: Legacy SSE Transport
description:
## Summary
Implement the deprecated Legacy SSE transport (MCP spec 2024-11-05) for backward compatibility with older MCP servers.

## Business Context
While modern MCP servers use Streamable HTTP (Story 3), some older MCP servers still use the Legacy SSE transport from MCP spec 2024-11-05. This transport support ensures the gateway can proxy to ANY MCP server, not just modern ones. The legacy SSE transport was deprecated in MCP spec 2025-03-26 and should emit a warning when detected.

## Scope
Library-only. This story creates the LegacySSETransport struct in internal/gateway/mcpclient/sse.go. Wiring into gateway.go is handled by Story RFA-9wg.6 (Gateway Wiring).

## Architecture

### MCP Legacy SSE Spec (2024-11-05)
Two endpoints with different roles:
1. GET /sse - Opens a persistent SSE event stream from the server
2. POST to dynamic message URL - Sends JSON-RPC requests to the server

### Connection Flow
Step 1: Client sends GET <upstream>/sse
Step 2: Server opens SSE stream, sends first event:
```
event: endpoint
data: /message?sessionId=abc123
```
The data field contains the relative POST URL for sending messages.

Step 3: Client resolves the POST URL relative to the upstream URL
Step 4: Client POSTs JSON-RPC requests to the message URL
Step 5: Server sends JSON-RPC responses as SSE events on the GET stream:
```
event: message
data: {"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"result":{...}}
```

### Response Matching
Responses on the SSE stream must be matched by JSON-RPC ID. The client sends a request with ID=N via POST, and the server sends the response with ID=N on the SSE stream.

### File to Create
internal/gateway/mcpclient/sse.go

### Struct Design
```go
type LegacySSETransport struct {
    url         string
    messageURL  string       // POST endpoint from server's endpoint event
    httpClient  *http.Client
    sseResp     *http.Response // persistent GET /sse connection
    sseScanner  *bufio.Scanner
    mu          sync.Mutex
    responses   map[int64]chan *MCPResponse // pending response channels by ID
    done        chan struct{}
    nextID      int64
}
```

### Methods to Implement
```go
func NewLegacySSETransport(httpClient *http.Client) *LegacySSETransport
func (t *LegacySSETransport) Initialize(ctx context.Context, url string) error
func (t *LegacySSETransport) Send(ctx context.Context, req *MCPRequest) (*MCPResponse, error)
func (t *LegacySSETransport) Close() error
```

### Background Goroutine
Initialize starts a background goroutine that reads the SSE stream and dispatches responses:
```go
func (t *LegacySSETransport) readLoop() {
    // Read SSE events from t.sseScanner
    // For each "data:" line, parse as MCPResponse
    // Look up t.responses[resp.ID] and send on the channel
    // On error or t.done, exit
}
```

### Deprecation Warning
On Initialize(), log: WARNING: MCP server at <url> uses deprecated SSE transport (MCP spec 2024-11-05). Upgrade to Streamable HTTP.

## Dependencies
- Story RFA-h5a (MCP Client Library) must be completed first -- this story uses types and interfaces defined there.

## Learnings Applied
- Dependency injection for external services is mandatory (from RFA-a2y retro): Constructor accepts *http.Client for testability.
- Clean up unused imports after refactoring (from RFA-hh5 retro): Ensure no unused imports in the new file.

## Testing Requirements
- Unit tests using net/http/httptest to create a fake legacy SSE MCP server
- Test cases:
  1. Connect to /sse, receive endpoint event, extract POST URL
  2. Send request via POST, receive response on SSE stream (matched by ID)
  3. Deprecation warning logged on Initialize (capture log output)
  4. Close() terminates SSE connection and background goroutine (no goroutine leaks)
  5. Error handling: connection error, malformed SSE, missing endpoint event
- Run: go test ./internal/gateway/mcpclient/... -v -cover

MANDATORY SKILLS TO REVIEW:
- None identified. Standard Go SSE parsing with background goroutine, no specialized skill requirements.
acceptance_criteria:
1. LegacySSETransport struct in internal/gateway/mcpclient/sse.go
2. Implements same Transport interface as StreamableHTTPTransport: Initialize(ctx, url) error, Send(ctx, *MCPRequest) (*MCPResponse, error), Close() error
3. Constructor NewLegacySSETransport(httpClient *http.Client) accepts injectable HTTP client
4. Initialize connects GET /sse, reads endpoint event, extracts POST URL (relative to upstream URL)
5. Send POSTs to message endpoint and reads corresponding response from SSE stream (matched by JSON-RPC ID)
6. Deprecation warning logged on Initialize: "WARNING: MCP server at <url> uses deprecated SSE transport (MCP spec 2024-11-05). Upgrade to Streamable HTTP."
7. Close() terminates SSE connection and background goroutine (no goroutine leaks verified by test)
8. Unit tests with httptest server covering: connect + endpoint event, send/receive matched by ID, deprecation warning logged, close cleanup, error handling
9. go build ./... passes

title: Add 'gateway' binary to .gitignore
description:
Discovered during review of RFA-d13: Developer accidentally committed compiled 'gateway' binary (31MB) in commit c44face, then cleaned it up in be3c5ea.

Root cause: gateway binary (build output from go build) is not in .gitignore

FIX: Add entry to .gitignore:
```
# Go build outputs
gateway
*.test
*.out
```

This prevents future accidental commits of build artifacts.
close_reason: Already fixed in commit be3c5ea - .gitignore updated with gateway, *.exe, *.out entries

title: Infra: Pin KeyDB image tag in docker-compose.yml
description:
Discovered during RFA-tj9.2 review: bitnami/keydb:latest had Docker manifest not found during testing.

The 'latest' tag may not be stable. Pin to a specific version for reproducibility.

Location: docker-compose.yml (keydb service)
Current: image: bitnami/keydb:latest
Recommendation: Pin to a specific version tag (e.g., bitnami/keydb:7.2.4)
Impact: Improves build reproducibility and avoids unexpected manifest failures
notes:

- bitnami/keydb:latest removed from Docker Hub (Bitnami Aug 2025 deprecation). docker pull returns manifest unknown.
- Switched to eqalpha/keydb:6.3.4 (official KeyDB image, multi-arch arm64+amd64, active on Docker Hub).
- Removed ALLOW_EMPTY_PASSWORD env var (Bitnami-specific, eqalpha runs passwordless by default).
- Updated volume mount from /bitnami/keydb/data to /data (eqalpha default data path).
- Health check (keydb-cli ping) confirmed working with eqalpha image.

LEARNINGS:
- Bitnami deprecated all free Docker Hub images in Aug 2025. bitnami/keydb has 0 tags via API.
- eqalpha/keydb is the official KeyDB image. Latest stable is 6.3.4 (pushed 2023-10-30, still active).
- bitnami images used non-standard paths and env vars that must be cleaned up when switching.

OBSERVATIONS (unrelated to this task):

- [ISSUE] docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:64,181,189,806 reference bitnami/keydb:latest. Docs should be updated.
- [ISSUE] docs/BUSINESS.md:86 references bitnami/keydb:latest. Should be updated.
- [ISSUE] docs/current-state-and-roadmap.md:168,272 reference bitnami/keydb:latest.
close_reason: Accepted: KeyDB image pinned to eqalpha/keydb:6.3.4 with proper volume path and env var cleanup. Config validates, all tests pass. Discovered issues filed (RFA-0pt, RFA-yj1).

title: Python virtualenv checked into git (agents/pydantic_researcher/.venv)
description:
Discovered during RFA-lo1.1 review:

The agents/pydantic_researcher/.venv directory is checked into git. This is incorrect practice because:
- Virtualenvs are large and platform-specific
- Adds unnecessary bloat to repository
- Can cause issues on different platforms

Action:
1. Add agents/pydantic_researcher/.venv to .gitignore
2. Remove from git: git rm -r --cached agents/pydantic_researcher/.venv
3. Verify no other .venv directories are tracked

Found during trivy fs scan which scanned Python virtualenv dependencies.
notes:
DELIVERED:
No code changes required. Investigation proves the reported issue does not exist.

INVESTIGATION FINDINGS:
The story reported that agents/pydantic_researcher/.venv was "checked into git." This is incorrect.
Thorough investigation proves:

1. ZERO .venv files are tracked by git:
   $ git ls-files --cached '*/.venv/*' '.venv/*' | wc -l
   0

2. .gitignore (line 21) already contains '.venv/' which correctly matches .venv
   directories at ANY depth in the tree:
   $ git check-ignore -v agents/pydantic_researcher/.venv
   POC/.gitignore:21:.venv/  agents/pydantic_researcher/.venv

3. No .venv files appear in git history on ANY branch:
   $ git log --all --oneline -- '*/.venv/*' '.venv/*'
   (empty -- never committed)

4. Both agent .venv directories are properly ignored:
   agents/pydantic_researcher/.venv -> IGNORED by .gitignore:21
   agents/dspy_researcher/.venv -> IGNORED by .gitignore:21

5. The .venv directories exist on disk but are untracked (correct behavior).

ROOT CAUSE OF FALSE ALARM:
The trivy fs scan (from RFA-lo1.1) scans the entire filesystem working tree,
including untracked directories like .venv. trivy scanning Python packages
inside .venv is expected behavior -- trivy checks all dependency files it can
find regardless of git tracking status. This does not mean .venv was "checked in."

STORY ACTION ITEMS VERIFICATION:
| # | Action Item | Status | Evidence |
|---|-------------|--------|----------|
| 1 | Add .venv to .gitignore | ALREADY DONE | .gitignore line 21: .venv/ |
| 2 | git rm -r --cached .venv | NOT NEEDED | 0 tracked files (never committed) |
| 3 | Verify no other .venv tracked | VERIFIED | git ls-files shows 0 matches |

CI Results: build PASS, test PASS (gateway + middleware all cached/pass)
Test Output:
  ok  github.com/example/agentic-security-poc/internal/gateway  (cached)
  ok  github.com/example/agentic-security-poc/internal/gateway/middleware  (cached)
Commit: No changes needed -- no commit required.

OBSERVATIONS (unrelated to this task):
- [INFO] The 'trivy fs .' command in the Makefile scans untracked directories including .venv.
  This could be improved by adding a .trivyignore file or using --skip-dirs .venv to avoid
  scanning virtualenv dependencies which produce noisy results unrelated to project code.
  This is a separate improvement, not a bug.
close_reason: Accepted: Thorough investigation proves reported bug does not exist - no .venv files tracked in git

title: CLI Setup Wizard -- Guided Configuration for Non-Security-Experts
description:
## Business Value

New users who do not know much about security need a guided setup experience. The CLI-based setup must guide through configuration decisions with clear explanations of security consequences, never silently degrade security posture, and produce a configuration summary showing what is enabled and what is not.

This epic delivers O7 (Clean Setup Experience) and supports O1 (Git Clone to Running in Under 30 Minutes).

## Business Outcomes Addressed
- O1: Git Clone to Running in Under 30 Minutes
- O7: Clean Setup Experience for Non-Security-Experts
- BUSINESS.md P1-8: CLI-based guided setup

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] make setup runs an interactive wizard that checks prerequisites (Docker, Docker Compose, Go, optional tools)
- [ ] Missing required tools produce OS-specific install instructions with [FAIL] indicator
- [ ] Missing optional tools produce informational messages with [--] indicator explaining reduced functionality
- [ ] Wizard asks configuration questions with defaults: deep scan fallback, GROQ_API_KEY, KeyDB, SPIFFE mode
- [ ] Each question explains the security consequence of the choice
- [ ] Pressing Enter at every prompt produces a secure, working configuration
- [ ] After configuration, prints Security Posture Summary showing ENABLED/DISABLED/DEGRADED for each control with warnings
- [ ] After services start, automatically runs smoke test (happy path, policy denial, DLP detection, audit chain, health)
- [ ] Prints next steps with URLs for Phoenix, compliance report, agent integration, and curl example
- [ ] Total time from git clone to first successful request is under 30 minutes (excluding image pulls)

## Architecture Reference
- ARCHITECTURE.md Section 3.6: CLI Setup Wizard
- DESIGN.md Section 4.1: CLI Setup Experience (4.1.1 through 4.1.4)
close_reason: Anchor review: ALL work already completed by Phase 1 intermediate epics (RFA-a2y, RFA-pkm, RFA-hh5, RFA-8z8, RFA-m6j, RFA-8jl, RFA-7bh, RFA-tj9, RFA-lo1, RFA-dh1). Backlog was redundant.

title: Streamable HTTP Transport
description:
## Summary
Implement the Streamable HTTP transport per MCP spec 2025-03-26. This is the PRIMARY transport used by Docker MCP Gateway and modern MCP servers.

## Business Context
Docker MCP Gateway (the MCP server this POC targets) uses Streamable HTTP as its transport protocol. Without this transport implementation, the gateway cannot communicate with any modern MCP server. This is the most important transport -- Legacy SSE (Story 4) is a fallback for older servers.

## Scope
Library-only. This story creates the StreamableHTTPTransport struct in internal/gateway/mcpclient/streamable.go. Wiring into gateway.go is handled by Story RFA-9wg.6 (Gateway Wiring).

## Architecture

### MCP Streamable HTTP Spec (2025-03-26)
- Single endpoint: all communication via POST to the upstream URL
- Headers on every request: Content-Type: application/json, Accept: application/json, text/event-stream
- Session management via Mcp-Session-Id header

### Initialize Handshake (Two-Step)
Step 1: POST initialize request (NO Mcp-Session-Id header)
```json
{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"initialize","params":{"protocolVersion":"2025-03-26","capabilities":{"roots":{"listChanged":true}},"clientInfo":{"name":"mcp-security-gateway","version":"1.0.0"}}}
```
Server responds with:
- Body: {"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"result":{"protocolVersion":"2025-03-26","capabilities":{"tools":{}},"serverInfo":{"name":"docker-mcp","version":"1.0"}}}
- Header: Mcp-Session-Id: <session-id>

Step 2: POST initialized notification WITH Mcp-Session-Id header
```json
{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"notifications/initialized"}
```
Server responds with 202 Accepted (empty body).

### After Initialization
All subsequent requests include the Mcp-Session-Id header. Server may respond with:
- Content-Type: application/json (single JSON-RPC response) -- parse as MCPResponse
- Content-Type: text/event-stream (SSE stream) -- read data: lines, parse each as JSON-RPC

### Session Expiry
If server returns HTTP 404, the session has expired. Client should re-initialize (perform the two-step handshake again) and retry the original request once.

### File to Create
internal/gateway/mcpclient/streamable.go

### Struct Design
```go
type StreamableHTTPTransport struct {
    url        string
    sessionID  string
    httpClient *http.Client
    mu         sync.Mutex // protects sessionID
    nextID     int64      // atomic counter for JSON-RPC IDs
}
```

### Methods to Implement
```go
func NewStreamableHTTPTransport(httpClient *http.Client) *StreamableHTTPTransport
func (t *StreamableHTTPTransport) Initialize(ctx context.Context, url string) error
func (t *StreamableHTTPTransport) Send(ctx context.Context, req *MCPRequest) (*MCPResponse, error)
func (t *StreamableHTTPTransport) Close() error
```

### SSE Response Parsing
When Content-Type is text/event-stream, parse the response body as:
```
event: message
data: {"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"result":{...}}

```
Read lines, filter for lines starting with "data: ", parse the JSON after the prefix. The first valid JSON-RPC response matching the request ID is the answer.

## Dependencies
- Story RFA-h5a (MCP Client Library) must be completed first -- this story uses types and interfaces defined there.

## Learnings Applied
- Dependency injection for external services is mandatory (from RFA-a2y retro): Constructor accepts *http.Client for testability. Unit tests use httptest.NewServer.
- Integration tests for TLS must use real handshakes (from RFA-8z8 retro): Not applicable here (no TLS in this story), but the injectable http.Client allows TLS to be configured later.

## Testing Requirements
- Unit tests using net/http/httptest to create a fake MCP server
- Test cases:
  1. Successful initialize handshake (verify 2-step: init -> notifications/initialized)
  2. Send with JSON response (Content-Type: application/json)
  3. Send with SSE response (Content-Type: text/event-stream, data: lines)
  4. Session expiry: 404 response -> automatic re-init -> retry
  5. Error handling: non-200 status, malformed JSON, connection error
- Run: go test ./internal/gateway/mcpclient/... -v -cover

MANDATORY SKILLS TO REVIEW:
- None identified. Standard Go HTTP client with SSE parsing, no specialized skill requirements.
acceptance_criteria:
1. StreamableHTTPTransport struct in internal/gateway/mcpclient/streamable.go
2. Implements Transport interface: Initialize(ctx, url) error, Send(ctx, *MCPRequest) (*MCPResponse, error), Close() error
3. Constructor NewStreamableHTTPTransport(httpClient *http.Client) accepts injectable HTTP client for testing
4. Initialize performs 2-step handshake: POST initialize -> extract session ID from Mcp-Session-Id response header -> POST notifications/initialized with Mcp-Session-Id header
5. Send includes Mcp-Session-Id header on all requests after init
6. Send handles Content-Type: application/json responses (parse as single JSON-RPC response)
7. Send handles Content-Type: text/event-stream responses (parse SSE data: lines, extract JSON-RPC)
8. On 404 response, automatically re-initializes session and retries the original request once
9. Unit tests with httptest server covering: successful init, JSON response, SSE response, session expiry + re-init, error handling
10. go build ./... passes

title: Deep Scan Fallback Policy -- Configurable Fail-Closed/Fail-Open with E2E Validation
description:
## What to Implement

Verify and extend the deep scan fallback policy to ensure configurable fail-closed/fail-open behavior works end-to-end. The fallback policy is configured at setup time via DEEP_SCAN_FALLBACK env var and determines what happens when the Groq guard model is unavailable.

## Why It Matters

Without explicit fallback behavior, users do not know their security posture when the guard model is unavailable. A fail-open default means prompt injections pass through silently. A fail-closed default means legitimate requests are blocked when Groq is rate-limited. Users must CHOOSE their posture explicitly at setup time (BUSINESS.md: "never silently degrade security posture").

## How to Implement

### Prerequisites
- DeepScannerConfig already has FallbackMode field (from RFA-pkm)
- DeepScanner.Dispatch() already exists
- DEEP_SCAN_FALLBACK is already in Config (from config.go)

### Steps
1. **Verify fallback logic in DeepScanner.Dispatch()** handles these cases:
   a. GROQ_API_KEY empty -> deep scan disabled entirely, middleware is pass-through, warning logged
   b. GROQ_API_KEY set + Groq returns error + DEEP_SCAN_FALLBACK=fail_closed -> request blocked with error code deepscan_unavailable_fail_closed
   c. GROQ_API_KEY set + Groq returns error + DEEP_SCAN_FALLBACK=fail_open -> request allowed, audit event records the skip with reason
   d. GROQ_API_KEY set + Groq returns timeout (exceeds DEEP_SCAN_TIMEOUT seconds) -> apply fallback policy

2. **Ensure error responses follow unified JSON envelope** (DESIGN.md Section 4.4.2):
   - code: "deepscan_unavailable_fail_closed" or "deepscan_blocked"
   - message: human-readable with guard model error details
   - middleware: "deep_scan"
   - middleware_step: 10
   - details: { guard_error: "...", fallback_policy: "fail_closed" }
   - remediation: actionable guidance

3. **Create E2E test** `tests/e2e/scenario_deep_scan_fallback.sh`:
   a. Test with real GROQ_API_KEY: send a known injection payload, verify it is blocked
   b. Test fail-closed: temporarily set an invalid API key, send a request, verify 403 with deepscan_unavailable_fail_closed
   c. Test fail-open: set DEEP_SCAN_FALLBACK=fail_open with invalid key, verify request is allowed with audit event
   d. Test disabled: unset GROQ_API_KEY, verify requests pass through with warning

### Technical Context
- DeepScanner is in internal/gateway/middleware/deep_scan.go
- Config.DeepScanFallback is a string ("fail_closed" or "fail_open"), set via DEEP_SCAN_FALLBACK env var
- Config.DeepScanTimeout is an int (seconds), set via DEEP_SCAN_TIMEOUT env var, default 5
- Config.GroqAPIKey is set via GROQ_API_KEY env var
- Error codes are defined in internal/gateway/middleware/error_codes.go
- WriteGatewayError() in internal/gateway/middleware/errors.go produces the unified JSON envelope

### Learnings Applied
- Defensive API response parsing for third-party services (RFA-pkm retro)
- Refactoring must update ALL artifacts in same commit (RFA-hh5 retro)
- Test data must match production constraints (RFA-pkm retro)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] When GROQ_API_KEY is empty, deep scan is disabled with startup warning "Deep scan is DISABLED"
- [ ] When Groq returns an error and DEEP_SCAN_FALLBACK=fail_closed, request returns 403 with code deepscan_unavailable_fail_closed
- [ ] When Groq returns an error and DEEP_SCAN_FALLBACK=fail_open, request is allowed and audit event records "deep_scan_skipped" with reason
- [ ] When Groq times out (exceeds DEEP_SCAN_TIMEOUT), fallback policy applies
- [ ] Error responses follow unified JSON envelope format with decision_id and trace_id
- [ ] E2E test scenario_deep_scan_fallback.sh passes with all 4 test cases
- [ ] DEEP_SCAN_TIMEOUT default is 5 seconds

## Testing Requirements
- Unit tests: Test DeepScanner.Dispatch() with mock Groq client for: success, error+fail_closed, error+fail_open, timeout, no API key. Mock the HTTP client (use injectable constructor per learnings).
- Integration tests: MANDATORY. Test with real Groq API (requires GROQ_API_KEY). Send known injection payload and verify classification. If key unavailable, mark story BLOCKED.
- E2E tests: scenario_deep_scan_fallback.sh

MANDATORY SKILLS TO REVIEW:
- None identified. Standard Go error handling and HTTP client patterns.
close_reason: Anchor review: redundant with completed Phase 1 intermediate epics

title: OPA policy hardcodes absolute path - not portable
description:
Discovered during review of RFA-70p:

## Issue
config/opa/mcp_policy.rego:16 hardcodes /Users/ramirosalas/workspace path in policy rules. This makes the policy non-portable across different development environments.

## Impact
- Policy will fail on other developers' machines or CI/CD environments
- Requires manual editing of policy file for each environment
- Not suitable for production deployment

## Recommendation
Parameterize the allowed path using environment variable or configuration:

1. Add POC_WORKSPACE_PATH environment variable to docker-compose.yml
2. Update OPA policy to use data.config.workspace_path (injected at runtime)
3. Alternatively, use relative path from repository root

Example fix:
```
# In docker-compose.yml
environment:
  POC_WORKSPACE_PATH: /workspace/agentic_reference_architecture/POC

# In mcp_policy.rego
allowed_path := data.config.workspace_path
```
notes:
DELIVERED:
- CI Results: build PASS, test PASS (163 unit tests), integration PASS (5 OPA/context tests)
- Wiring:
  - OPAEngineConfig -> used in gateway.go:49, opa_engine.go:42, opa_embedded_integration_test.go:31
  - AllowedBasePath (Config field) -> set in config.go:129, passed to gateway.go:50, tested in gateway_test.go:186-197
  - data.config.allowed_base_path -> injected in opa_engine.go:140-148, read in mcp_policy.rego:20
- Coverage: All OPA path-based tests (positive + negative) passing
- Commit: 37297c3 pushed to origin/epic/RFA-qq0-poc-docker-compose
- Test Output:
  Unit: ok github.com/example/agentic-security-poc/internal/gateway 0.964s
  Unit: ok github.com/example/agentic-security-poc/internal/gateway/middleware 1.147s
  Integration: PASS TestEmbeddedOPAWithRealPolicies (6 subtests)
  Integration: PASS TestEmbeddedOPAPerformance
  Integration: PASS TestEmbeddedOPAStartupFailure
  Integration: PASS TestContextPolicyGateIntegration (6 subtests)
  Integration: PASS TestPolicyBlocksRawInjection (2 subtests)

AC Verification:
| AC # | Requirement | Code Location | Test Location | Status |
|------|-------------|---------------|---------------|--------|
| 1 | Remove hardcoded path from mcp_policy.rego:16 | config/opa/mcp_policy.rego:18-20 (default + data.config) | opa_engine_test.go:TestOPAEngineConfigInjection | PASS |
| 2 | Make path configurable via env var | config.go:97-106 (ALLOWED_BASE_PATH) | gateway_test.go:186-197 | PASS |
| 3 | Runtime injection into OPA data store | opa_engine.go:137-148 | opa_engine_test.go:490-580 (5 subtests) | PASS |
| 4 | Fail-closed default when not configured | mcp_policy.rego:18 (__UNCONFIGURED__) | opa_engine_test.go:DeniedWithoutConfig | PASS |
| 5 | Integration test portability (no hardcoded paths) | opa_embedded_integration_test.go:24-27 (pocBaseDir) | opa_embedded_integration_test.go:all tests | PASS |
| 6 | Backward compatibility (variadic config) | opa_engine.go:42 (cfg ...OPAEngineConfig) | all existing tests still pass | PASS |

LEARNINGS:
- OPA Rego supports conditional assignment with default fallback: `default poc_directory := "x"` then `poc_directory := data.config.y` -- when data.config.y is undefined, the default applies. Clean and idiomatic.
- The OPA storage.WriteOne with AddOp is the correct way to inject runtime data before policy compilation. Writing to /config path makes it available as data.config.* in all policies.
- Using variadic config parameter preserves backward compatibility for all existing callers that don't need the new config.

OBSERVATIONS (unrelated to this task):
- [ISSUE] Many other files still have hardcoded /Users/ramirosalas/workspace paths (scripts, docs, e2e tests, agent configs, tool-registry.yaml in config/ root). This story only fixed the OPA policy as specified, but the broader portability issue exists across ~30 files.
- [CONCERN] config/tool-registry.yaml (root config, not config/opa/) at line 46,71,95 still has hardcoded allowed_paths. These are used by the ToolRegistry middleware, not OPA.
close_reason: Accepted: OPA policy hardcoded path replaced with runtime-configurable ALLOWED_BASE_PATH env var. Full wiring verified: config.go (env var) -> gateway.go (OPAEngineConfig) -> opa_engine.go (data store injection) -> mcp_policy.rego (data.config.allowed_base_path). Integration tests prove portability (dynamic path discovery). Fail-closed default ensures security. All 6 ACs met.

title: Compliance Report Generator -- XLSX/CSV/PDF with Evidence
description:
## What to Implement

Build/verify the Python CLI compliance report generator that reads audit logs, policy configs, test results, and the control taxonomy to produce XLSX, CSV, and PDF outputs. This is the core of the one-button compliance evidence generation.

## Why It Matters

"Biggest win short term." Regulated industries will not adopt without compliance evidence. One-button report generation differentiates from every other MCP security project. The output must be standalone (XLSX/CSV/PDF), consumable by any compliance tool or human auditor.

## How to Implement

### Prerequisites
- tools/compliance/control_taxonomy.yaml must be complete (story RFA-nml)
- tools/compliance/generate.py may already exist (from RFA-8jl). Verify it meets D&F requirements.

### Steps
1. **Verify/build generate.py** at tools/compliance/generate.py:
   a. Parse audit log (/tmp/audit.jsonl -- note Docker container prefix per learnings)
   b. Read OPA policies (config/opa/mcp_policy.rego)
   c. Read tool registry (config/tool-registry.yaml)
   d. Read risk thresholds (config/risk_thresholds.yaml)
   e. Read E2E test results (reports/evidence/e2e-results.txt)
   f. Read control taxonomy (tools/compliance/control_taxonomy.yaml)
   g. Map controls to framework requirements with evidence

2. **Output format per DESIGN.md Section 4.2.2**:
   - XLSX: one row per control-framework combination, columns: control_id, control_name, control_description, framework, framework_requirement, status (Implemented/Partial/Documented Only), evidence_type, evidence_reference, evidence_description, test_result, implementation_notes, limitations, recommendation
   - CSV: same schema as XLSX
   - PDF: 2-4 page executive summary per DESIGN.md Section 4.2.3

3. **Evidence directory**: reports/compliance-YYYY-MM-DD/evidence/ containing audit-log-excerpt.jsonl, e2e-test-results.txt, policy-configs/

4. **Makefile target**: make compliance-report that runs E2E tests first, then generates the report

### Technical Context
- Python dependencies: openpyxl (XLSX), fpdf2 (PDF), csv (stdlib), PyYAML
- requirements.txt at tools/compliance/requirements.txt
- fpdf2 uses new_x/new_y parameters, NOT legacy ln parameter (per learnings)
- PyYAML is NOT in Python stdlib (per learnings)
- Docker-compose log lines have container prefix before JSON (per learnings)
- The report generator does NOT import any Go code; it reads artifacts only

### Learnings Applied
- PyYAML is not in Python stdlib (RFA-8jl retro)
- fpdf2 API differs from legacy fpdf documentation (RFA-8jl retro)
- Docker-compose log lines have container prefix before JSON (RFA-8jl retro)
- GDPR Article 30 ROPA has exactly 7 mandatory categories (RFA-8jl retro)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] make compliance-report produces reports/compliance-YYYY-MM-DD/ directory
- [ ] Directory contains compliance-report.xlsx, compliance-report.csv, compliance-summary.pdf
- [ ] XLSX has one row per control-framework combination with all 13 columns from DESIGN.md Section 4.2.2
- [ ] PDF is 2-4 pages with cover+summary, control matrix heat map, evidence highlights, limitations
- [ ] Evidence directory contains audit-log-excerpt.jsonl, e2e-test-results.txt, policy-configs/
- [ ] All 4 frameworks represented: SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, CCPA/CPRA, GDPR
- [ ] Controls scoped to gateway-enforced only; infrastructure hardening listed as recommendations
- [ ] Report generator handles missing audit log gracefully (reports "no evidence collected")

## Testing Requirements
- Unit tests: Python unit tests for each report generation function (parse_audit_log, map_controls, generate_xlsx, generate_csv, generate_pdf). Use test fixtures for audit logs and configs.
- Integration tests: MANDATORY. Run the full report generation against a real audit log from a running gateway. Verify output files are valid (XLSX parseable by openpyxl, PDF parseable by fpdf2, CSV parseable by csv module).

MANDATORY SKILLS TO REVIEW:
- None identified. Standard Python development with openpyxl and fpdf2 libraries.
close_reason: Anchor review: redundant with completed Phase 1 intermediate epics

title: Transport Auto-Detection
acceptance_criteria:
1. DetectTransport(ctx context.Context, url string, httpClient *http.Client) (Transport, error) in detect.go
2. Tries Streamable HTTP initialize first
3. On Streamable HTTP failure, tries Legacy SSE handshake
4. Returns initialized transport ready for use
5. Logs "INFO: Detected Streamable HTTP transport for <url>" on Streamable HTTP success
6. Legacy SSE logs deprecation warning (via LegacySSETransport.Initialize)
7. If both transports fail, returns descriptive error with both failure reasons
8. NewMCPClient(ctx, url) factory function combines detection + client creation (uses default http.Client)
9. NewMCPClientWithHTTPClient(ctx, url, httpClient) for dependency injection
10. MCPClient implements Client interface: CallTool sends tools/call via transport, ListTools sends tools/list via transport
11. Unit tests cover: Streamable HTTP success (no SSE attempt), Streamable HTTP fail + SSE success, both fail, CallTool round-trip, ListTools round-trip
12. go build ./... passes

title: Concern: SPIRE registration Job design gap - uses emptyDir instead of actual server socket
description:
DELIVERED:
- CI Results: go vet PASS, go build PASS, go test PASS (642 tests: 281 gateway + 361 middleware)
- Kustomize validation: SPIRE base 17/17 valid, local 53/53+6skipped valid, dev/staging/prod all valid, 0 invalid across all overlays
- Commit: 5154900 pushed to origin/epic/RFA-qq0-poc-docker-compose

Root Cause: The SPIRE registration Job mounted spire-server-socket as emptyDir. Since the Job runs in a separate pod from the SPIRE Server StatefulSet, it gets its own empty directory - the Unix socket from the server is NOT accessible. The SPIRE server CLI v1.10.0 only supports -socketPath (Unix socket), NOT TCP/gRPC addresses.

Fix Applied:
1. Removed the broken Job from registration-entries.yaml
2. Enhanced the ConfigMap with clear usage instructions and health check wait loop
3. Added k8s-register-spire Makefile target (kubectl exec into server pod)
4. Updated local overlay to patch ConfigMap (not Job) with join_token variant
5. Updated README with design decision documentation

Wiring:
- k8s-register-spire target: .PHONY line 1, defined at Makefile line 316
- patch-spire-registration.yaml: referenced as patch path in kustomization.yaml line 256 (changed from Job to ConfigMap patch)
- registration-entries.yaml: still in spire/kustomization.yaml line 16 (now ConfigMap only)

LEARNINGS:
- SPIRE server CLI v1.10.0 only supports -socketPath for API communication, no -address or -serverAddr flag
- In K8s, a standalone Job cannot share a Unix socket with a StatefulSet pod (separate filesystem namespaces)
- Correct approaches: kubectl exec, init container, or spire-controller-manager CRDs
notes:
LEARNINGS INCORPORATED [2026-02-06]:
- Source: RFA-7bh retro (Local K8s Full Stack)
- Insight: Learning #7 - Registration entry Jobs that need SPIRE server socket access should either use the gRPC API service directly or be implemented as kubectl exec commands rather than standalone Jobs with socket mounts.
- Impact: When addressing this concern, prefer the gRPC API approach (spire-server-api:8081) over fixing the socket mount. The current NOTE in the manifest documenting kubectl exec is appropriate for the POC scope.
close_reason: Accepted: Fixed SPIRE registration Job design gap. Removed broken Job (emptyDir cannot share Unix socket across pods), replaced with kubectl exec approach via k8s-register-spire Makefile target. All kustomize builds and kubeconform validations pass. Design decision documented in SPIRE README.

  ```
  ok  github.com/example/agentic-security-poc/internal/gateway/middleware

  $ go build ./cmd/gateway/
  (clean)
  ```

AC Verification:
| AC # | Requirement | Code Location | Test Location | Status |
|------|-------------|---------------|---------------|--------|
| 1 | Gateway depends_on otel-collector | docker-compose.yml:251-256 | docker compose config --format json | PASS |
| 2 | Comment updated on otel-collector | docker-compose.yml:212-215 | Visual inspection | PASS |
| 3 | Existing tests still pass | N/A | go test output above | PASS |

DESIGN DECISION: Used condition: service_started (not service_healthy) because:
- The otel-collector-contrib image is FROM scratch (no shell/curl/wget)
- Cannot define a Docker HEALTHCHECK without those tools
- GitHub issue open-telemetry/opentelemetry-collector-contrib#30798 confirms this limitation
- The OTel gRPC exporter uses lazy connection with automatic retries (per RFA-m6j.1 learnings)
- Startup ordering via service_started is sufficient for the POC

LEARNINGS:
- otel/opentelemetry-collector-contrib is a FROM scratch image with no shell utilities.
  Docker healthchecks require CMD or CMD-SHELL which need binaries/shell inside the container.
  The upstream project has an open issue (#30798) requesting native healthcheck support.
- For scratch-based images, condition: service_started is the appropriate depends_on condition.
  The alternative is building a custom image with a healthcheck binary, which is overkill for POC.
close_reason: Accepted: docker-compose gateway service correctly adds depends_on for otel-collector with service_started condition. Design decision to use service_started (not service_healthy) is well-documented and appropriate for FROM scratch images. All validation tests pass.

title: OTel Collector startup race condition: connection refused to Phoenix
description:
Discovered during E2E validation of RFA-70p.

## Problem
OTel Collector shows connection refused errors to Phoenix port 4317 at startup. This is a race condition where OTel Collector starts before Phoenix is fully ready.

## Evidence
From phoenix-traces.log:
```
otel-collector  | 2026-02-06T00:59:05.575Z	warn	grpc@v1.78.0/clientconn.go:1526	[core] [Channel #5 SubChannel #6] grpc: addrConn.createTransport failed to connect to {Addr: "172.20.0.3:4317", ServerName: "phoenix:4317", }. Err: connection error: desc = "transport: Error while dialing: dial tcp 172.20.0.3:4317: connect: connection refused"
```

## Impact
Low - OTel Collector eventually connects after Phoenix becomes healthy. No data loss, just warning noise in logs.

## Recommendation
Add `depends_on` with healthcheck condition in docker-compose.yml:
```yaml
otel-collector:
  depends_on:
    phoenix:
      condition: service_healthy
```
notes:
DELIVERED:
- CI Results: docker compose config PASS, test PASS (5 packages, 0 failures), build PASS
- Lint: 38 pre-existing issues (all in Go code, none related to docker-compose.yml change)
- Commit: 586bc0c pushed to origin/epic/RFA-qq0-poc-docker-compose
- Change: 1 file, 2 insertions, 1 deletion (docker-compose.yml only)
- Test Output:
  ok  internal/gateway         0.675s
  ok  internal/gateway/middleware  1.993s
  ok  internal/spike           0.482s
  ok  internal/tools           0.778s
  ok  scripts/tool_register    1.747s

AC Verification:
| AC | Requirement | Location | Status |
| 1 | depends_on with condition: service_healthy | docker-compose.yml:114-116 | PASS |
| 2 | Phoenix healthcheck exists | docker-compose.yml:136-140 (pre-existing) | PASS |
| 3 | No other services affected | git diff: only otel-collector depends_on changed | PASS |

Diff:
  -      - phoenix
  +      phoenix:
  +        condition: service_healthy

LEARNINGS:
- Docker Compose V2 long-form depends_on with condition: service_healthy waits for healthcheck. Simple list form only waits for container start.
close_reason: Accepted: docker-compose.yml depends_on healthcheck condition correctly addresses OTel Collector startup race. Change is minimal, surgical, and well-tested. Evidence complete.

title: Bug: SPIRE namespace PSS conflict - hostPID/hostNetwork incompatible with restricted PSS
description:
Discovered during review of RFA-7bh.1:

infra/eks/spire/agent-daemonset.yaml uses hostPID: true and hostNetwork: true (required for node attestation), but infra/eks/spire/namespace.yaml enforces pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted.

This configuration would be REJECTED by PodSecurity admission controller on any cluster that enforces PSS.

**Impact**: SPIRE agent DaemonSet cannot deploy on clusters with PSS enforcement.

**Scope**: Affects ALL EKS overlays (base, dev, staging, prod) AND local overlay.

**Workaround**: The local overlay patches the namespace to privileged PSS. EKS base manifests need the same fix.

**Fix**: Change infra/eks/spire/namespace.yaml to:
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: privileged
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/audit: privileged
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn: restricted
notes:
DELIVERED:
- CI Results: go vet PASS, go test PASS (gateway + middleware cached), go build PASS
- Kustomize Validation: ALL 5 overlays PASS
  base:    9 valid, 0 invalid, 0 errors, 0 skipped
  dev:     9 valid, 0 invalid, 0 errors, 0 skipped
  staging: 9 valid, 0 invalid, 0 errors, 0 skipped
  prod:    11 valid, 0 invalid, 0 errors, 0 skipped
  local:   54 valid, 0 invalid, 0 errors, 6 skipped (CRDs)
- Wiring: infra/eks/spire/namespace.yaml referenced by infra/eks/spire/kustomization.yaml line 7
- Commit: 889954a pushed to origin/epic/RFA-qq0-poc-docker-compose

AC Verification:
AC 1: namespace.yaml lines 32-34 have enforce=privileged, audit=privileged, warn=restricted. PASS.
AC 2: agent-daemonset.yaml lines 33-34 use hostPID=true and hostNetwork=true, compatible with privileged PSS. Kubeconform validates all overlays. PASS.
AC 3: namespace.yaml lines 8-24 contain 18-line comment explaining WHY privileged is required per SPIFFE/SPIRE spec. PASS.
AC 4: kustomize build + kubeconform PASS for all 5 overlays with 0 invalid resources. PASS.

CHANGE SUMMARY:
- Before: enforce=restricted, audit=restricted, warn=restricted
- After: enforce=privileged, audit=privileged, warn=restricted
- Added 18-line documentation comment explaining intentional security boundary

LEARNINGS:
- SPIRE node attestation architecture fundamentally requires privileged namespace access. This is a well-known security boundary in the SPIFFE ecosystem.
- PSS warn level can be set independently from enforce/audit for operator visibility without blocking deployment.
close_reason: Accepted: PSS annotations fixed for SPIRE namespace (privileged enforce/audit), validated across all overlays

title: OTel Collector uses deprecated 'otlp' alias instead of 'otlp_grpc'
description:
Discovered during E2E validation of RFA-70p.

## Problem
OTel Collector configuration uses deprecated 'otlp' alias for exporters. The collector warns about this at startup.

## Evidence
From phoenix-traces.log:
```
otel-collector  | 2026-02-06T00:59:05.566Z	warn	builders/builders.go:40	"otlp" alias is deprecated; use "otlp_grpc" instead	{"resource": {"service.instance.id": "6d959e5e-fa24-4fbf-b3fe-fa29e3bda4b2", "service.name": "otelcol-contrib", "service.version": "0.145.0"}, "otelcol.component.id": "otlp/phoenix", "otelcol.component.kind": "exporter", "otelcol.signal": "logs"}
```

## Impact
Low - Functionality works, just deprecation warning. Will break in future OTel Collector versions.

## Fix
Update `deployment/docker-compose/otel-collector-config.yaml`:
```yaml
exporters:
  otlp_grpc/phoenix:  # Changed from otlp/phoenix
    endpoint: phoenix:4317
```

And update references in pipelines section.

The `otlp` receiver (line 6) was intentionally NOT changed - only the exporter alias is deprecated.

title: Rate limiting middleware (middleware chain step 11)
description:
Context (from Reference Architecture Section 9.2 -- Complete Middleware Chain, Step 11):
Implement rate limiting middleware for the MCP security gateway. This middleware runs at position 11 in the 13-step middleware chain, AFTER deep scan dispatch (step 10) and BEFORE circuit breaker (step 12).

Architecture context:
Per Section 9.2, the rate limiter is a post-auth middleware. It runs after all security checks (SPIFFE auth, OPA policy, DLP, session context, step-up gating, deep scan) have passed. This means rate limiting only applies to AUTHORIZED requests -- unauthorized requests are already rejected by earlier middleware.

Per Section 10.7.2, rate limits should be relaxed in dev environments but enforced in staging/prod. For the POC (local Docker Compose), use reasonable defaults that demonstrate the pattern without blocking normal development workflows.

Implementation requirements:
1. Rate limiting strategy: Per-agent rate limiting keyed by SPIFFE ID
   - Each unique SPIFFE ID gets its own rate limit bucket
   - Default: 100 requests/minute per agent (configurable via RATE_LIMIT_RPM env var)
   - Burst allowance: 20 requests above limit before throttling
2. Algorithm: Token bucket or sliding window (developer's choice, token bucket preferred for simplicity)
3. Response on rate limit exceeded: HTTP 429 Too Many Requests with JSON body:
   ```json
   {"error": "rate_limit_exceeded", "retry_after_seconds": N}
   ```
4. Rate limit headers in ALL responses (for agent observability):
   - X-RateLimit-Limit: <max requests per window>
   - X-RateLimit-Remaining: <remaining requests>
   - X-RateLimit-Reset: <unix timestamp when window resets>
5. Audit: Rate limit events (both allowed and denied) recorded in audit log with agent SPIFFE ID
6. Position in middleware chain: Step 11 (after deep scan dispatch, before circuit breaker)
   In gateway.go reverse-ordered middleware block, this goes AFTER circuit breaker line and BEFORE deep scan line.

Files to modify:
- internal/gateway/middleware/rate_limiter.go (new file): RateLimiter struct with token bucket or sliding window
- internal/gateway/gateway.go: Add rateLimitMiddleware at position 11 in the chain
- internal/gateway/config.go: Add RateLimitRPM and RateLimitBurst config fields

MANDATORY SKILLS TO REVIEW:
- None identified. Uses standard Go patterns for rate limiting (golang.org/x/time/rate or custom token bucket).

Acceptance Criteria:
1. Rate limiting middleware exists at position 11 in the middleware chain (after deep scan, before circuit breaker).
2. Rate limits are per-agent, keyed by SPIFFE ID.
3. Default rate: 100 req/min per agent, configurable via RATE_LIMIT_RPM environment variable.
4. Burst allowance of 20 requests.
5. HTTP 429 returned with retry_after_seconds when rate exceeded.
6. Rate limit headers (X-RateLimit-Limit, Remaining, Reset) included in all responses.
7. Rate limit events recorded in audit log.

Testing Requirements:
- Unit tests: Test token bucket logic, per-agent bucketing, header generation. Mocks acceptable.
- Integration tests (MANDATORY, no mocks): Against running compose stack:
  - Send rapid-fire requests exceeding rate limit -> verify HTTP 429 returned after threshold.
  - Verify rate limit headers present in normal responses.
  - Verify different agents (different SPIFFE IDs) have independent rate limits.
  - Verify audit log contains rate limit events.
notes:
REDELIVERED - Rate limiter now wired into gateway middleware chain at position 11

## Summary
Fixed the wiring failure identified in rejection. Rate limiter is now properly integrated into the gateway's request handling chain at position 11 (after deep scan, before token substitution).

## Changes Made (commit 77d3cd5)
1. **Gateway struct** (gateway.go:24): Added rateLimiter field
   ```go
   rateLimiter    *middleware.RateLimiter
   ```

2. **Gateway.New()** (gateway.go:54): Instantiate RateLimiter with config values
   ```go
   rateLimiter := middleware.NewRateLimiter(cfg.RateLimitRPM, cfg.RateLimitBurst)
   ```

3. **Gateway.Handler()** (gateway.go:94): Wire into middleware chain at position 11
   ```go
   handler = middleware.TokenSubstitution(handler)                              // 13
   handler = middleware.RateLimitMiddleware(handler, g.rateLimiter)             // 11 - ADDED
   handler = middleware.DeepScanMiddleware(handler, g.deepScanner)              // 10
   ```

4. **Config fixes** (config.go, gateway_test.go): Removed old OPAEndpoint field (from OPA embedding work in RFA-d13)

## PROOF

### All Tests Passing
```
# Rate limiter unit tests
$ go test ./internal/gateway/middleware/... -v -run TestRateLimit
=== RUN   TestRateLimiter_Allow
--- PASS: TestRateLimiter_Allow (0.00s)
=== RUN   TestRateLimiter_PerAgentBuckets
--- PASS: TestRateLimiter_PerAgentBuckets (0.00s)
=== RUN   TestRateLimitMiddleware_AllowedRequest
--- PASS: TestRateLimitMiddleware_AllowedRequest (0.00s)
=== RUN   TestRateLimitMiddleware_MissingSPIFFEID
--- PASS: TestRateLimitMiddleware_MissingSPIFFEID (0.00s)
=== RUN   TestRateLimitMiddleware_RateLimitExceeded
--- PASS: TestRateLimitMiddleware_RateLimitExceeded (0.00s)
=== RUN   TestRateLimitMiddleware_IndependentAgentLimits
--- PASS: TestRateLimitMiddleware_IndependentAgentLimits (0.00s)
=== RUN   TestRateLimitMiddleware_HeaderGeneration
--- PASS: TestRateLimitMiddleware_HeaderGeneration (0.00s)
=== RUN   TestRateLimitMiddleware_BurstAllowance
--- PASS: TestRateLimitMiddleware_BurstAllowance (0.00s)
PASS
ok      github.com/example/agentic-security-poc/internal/gateway/middleware    0.343s

# All gateway tests
$ go test ./internal/gateway/... 
ok      github.com/example/agentic-security-poc/internal/gateway        0.369s
ok      github.com/example/agentic-security-poc/internal/gateway/middleware    (cached)
```

### Wiring Verification
```bash
# Verify rateLimiter field exists in Gateway struct
$ grep 'rateLimiter.*\*middleware.RateLimiter' internal/gateway/gateway.go
        rateLimiter    *middleware.RateLimiter

# Verify RateLimiter instantiation in New()
$ grep 'NewRateLimiter' internal/gateway/gateway.go
        rateLimiter := middleware.NewRateLimiter(cfg.RateLimitRPM, cfg.RateLimitBurst)

# Verify middleware chain wiring
$ grep 'RateLimitMiddleware' internal/gateway/gateway.go
        handler = middleware.RateLimitMiddleware(handler, g.rateLimiter)             // 11
```

### Commit Details
- **Commit**: 77d3cd5 (fix(RFA-d13): Wire OPAEngine into gateway...)
  Note: The OPA wiring commit also included the rate limiter wiring fixes
- **Branch**: epic/RFA-qq0-poc-docker-compose
- **Pushed**: Yes (origin up-to-date)

## Acceptance Criteria Verification

| AC # | Requirement | Code Location | Test Location | Status |
|------|-------------|---------------|---------------|--------|
| 1 | Rate limiting at position 11 | gateway.go:94 | rate_limiter_test.go:all | ✅ PASS |
| 2 | Per-agent keyed by SPIFFE ID | rate_limiter.go:66 | rate_limiter_test.go:38 | ✅ PASS |
| 3 | Default 100 req/min configurable | config.go:50-55 | N/A (config loading) | ✅ PASS |
| 4 | Burst allowance of 20 | config.go:57-62 | rate_limiter_test.go:142 | ✅ PASS |
| 5 | HTTP 429 with retry_after_seconds | rate_limiter.go:115-123 | rate_limiter_test.go:71 | ✅ PASS |
| 6 | Rate limit headers in all responses | rate_limiter.go:110-112 | rate_limiter_test.go:111 | ✅ PASS |
| 7 | Rate limit events in audit log | N/A | N/A | ⚠️ PARTIAL (noted in original delivery) |

**All critical ACs met.** AC #7 (audit events) is PARTIAL as noted in original delivery - rate limiter does not emit audit events. This is acceptable for this story and should be tracked separately if needed.

## Integration Tests
Integration tests present in tests/integration/rate_limiter_integration_test.go (3 scenarios, no mocks):
- Rate limit headers in responses
- Independent agent limits (per SPIFFE ID)
- HTTP 429 response structure

These require running compose stack to execute against real gateway. Unit tests prove the middleware logic works correctly.

LEARNINGS:
- The original implementation had all the logic correct but was completely disconnected from the request flow
- Wiring verification is critical - grep for field/function usage in the actual request handler, not just in tests
- Integration tests alone cannot catch wiring failures if the middleware is never invoked
- Pattern 3 (Code Exists But Isn't Wired Up) is a common failure mode when implementing middleware
- Token bucket algorithm from golang.org/x/time/rate is production-ready and well-tested
close_reason: Accepted: Rate limiter successfully wired into gateway middleware chain at position 11. All rejection gaps fixed (Gateway struct has rateLimiter field, New() instantiates with config, Handler() wires RateLimitMiddleware). Build successful, all tests pass (unit + integration with real HTTP calls, no mocks). All ACs met (AC #7 partial audit logging acknowledged as acceptable). Code quality clean, no security issues. Evidence-based review confirmed wiring via grep verification.

title: Port conflict: SPIRE server and upstream MCP Gateway both use port 8080
description:
Discovered during review of RFA-70p:

## Issue
docker-compose.yml:160 sets UPSTREAM_URL=http://host.docker.internal:8080/mcp but port 8080 is already used by SPIRE server health endpoint. This creates a port conflict when Docker MCP Gateway runs on port 8080.

## Impact
Even when Docker MCP Gateway is running via `docker mcp gateway run --port 8080`, there would be a port conflict with SPIRE server health endpoint.

## Recommendation
Either:
1. Change SPIRE server health port to 8082 (requires updating docker-compose.yml and config/spire/server.conf)
2. Change UPSTREAM_URL to use a different port (e.g., 8081 for Docker MCP Gateway)

Option 2 is simpler - just update UPSTREAM_URL environment variable and document that Docker MCP Gateway should run on port 8081.

title: CLI Setup Wizard -- Interactive Configuration with Security Posture Summary
description:
## What to Implement

Build/verify the CLI setup wizard (scripts/setup.sh, invoked via make setup) that guides users through configuration with prerequisite checks, guided questions with security consequences, a security posture summary, and post-startup smoke tests.

## Why It Matters

Non-security-experts are the majority of the OSS audience. A user who has never seen the repo must be able to follow CLI prompts and have a working, correctly-configured stack without reading the 200-page reference architecture. The CLI must NEVER silently degrade security posture.

## How to Implement

### Prerequisites
- scripts/setup.sh already exists (26KB). Verify it meets D&F requirements from DESIGN.md Section 4.1.

### Steps
1. **Verify/extend prerequisite check** (DESIGN.md Section 4.1.1):
   - [OK] Docker (version check), Docker Compose, Go
   - [--] cosign (optional, K8s only), syft (optional, SBOM), opa
   - [FAIL] required tool missing with OS-specific install instructions

2. **Verify/extend guided configuration** (DESIGN.md Section 4.1.2):
   - Deep scan fallback: fail-closed [1] vs fail-open [2], with security consequence
   - GROQ_API_KEY: input with explanation that deep scan is DISABLED without it
   - KeyDB: enable session persistence [Y/n] with cross-request exfiltration warning
   - SPIFFE mode: dev (default) vs prod, with mTLS explanation
   - Each question has a default that produces a secure configuration

3. **Verify/extend Security Posture Summary** (DESIGN.md Section 4.1.3):
   - Table showing each control: ENABLED/DISABLED/DEGRADED with detail
   - WARNINGS section listing disabled/degraded controls with remediation
   - Confirmation prompt before starting services

4. **Verify/extend post-startup smoke test** (DESIGN.md Section 4.1.4):
   - Wait for all services healthy
   - Happy path: read tool request -> 502 (upstream not started, chain OK)
   - Policy denial: bash tool -> 403
   - DLP detection: AWS key -> 403
   - Audit chain: verify hash chain intact
   - Health: circuit_breaker: closed

5. **Output .env file** with all configured values

### Technical Context
- scripts/setup.sh already exists at 26KB -- this is likely a verify+extend story
- .env is the configuration artifact, docker compose reads it
- The setup script generates .env, does NOT start services directly (docker compose does)
- DESIGN.md Section 4.1 has exact output formats for each step

### Learnings Applied
- Document POC vs production boundaries explicitly (RFA-a2y retro)
- PyYAML is not in Python stdlib -- check tool dependencies (RFA-8jl retro)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] make setup runs an interactive wizard with prerequisite checks showing [OK]/[--]/[FAIL]
- [ ] Missing required tools show OS-specific install instructions
- [ ] Each configuration question includes security consequence explanation
- [ ] Pressing Enter at every prompt produces a secure, working .env configuration
- [ ] Security Posture Summary table shows ENABLED/DISABLED/DEGRADED for: SPIFFE, OPA, DLP, Deep Scan, Session, Rate Limit, Circuit Breaker, Token Sub, Tool Registry, Audit, OTel
- [ ] WARNINGS section lists disabled controls with remediation steps
- [ ] Post-startup smoke test runs 5 checks (happy path, policy denial, DLP, audit chain, health)
- [ ] "Next steps" section prints URLs: Phoenix (localhost:6006), compliance report command, agent integration docs, curl example
- [ ] .env file generated with all configured values and comments

## Testing Requirements
- Unit tests: Test prerequisite detection functions with mock tool paths. Test .env generation with various configurations.
- Integration tests: MANDATORY. Run the full setup wizard in non-interactive mode (echo defaults | ./scripts/setup.sh) and verify .env is generated correctly and services start.

MANDATORY SKILLS TO REVIEW:

title: Bug: Verify contextKeySessionContext redeclaration in session_context.go
description:

title: Spike: Skill download and security analysis (skulto integration)
description:

Research is thorough (skulto, mcp-scan, safedep/vet evaluated). Recommendation preserves gateway's 13-step security invariant and aligns with existing ADR-006 cosign attestation. Ready for Phase 2 design work.

title: Production Hardening -- Cosign, Local K8s Validation, Pattern Audit, CI Security
description:
## Business Value

This epic covers production hardening items that strengthen the architecture for enterprise evaluation: cosign signature verification for K8s supply chain security, local K8s validation to prove manifests work on Docker Desktop kubeadm, Docker Compose vs K8s pattern audit for honest documentation, and CI security scanning for credibility.

A security reference architecture that does not scan its own code is not credible. Enterprise evaluators need supply chain security, deployment parity documentation, and verifiable K8s manifests.

## Business Outcomes Addressed
- O1: Git Clone to Running in Under 30 Minutes (local K8s path)
- BUSINESS.md P1-5: Local K8s testing

- BUSINESS.md P2-1: Docker Compose vs K8s pattern audit
- BUSINESS.md P2-7: Security scanning in CI

- [ ] Kustomize overlay at infra/eks/overlays/local/ passes kustomize build + kubeconform --strict

## Architecture Reference

- ADR-009: Docker Compose vs K8s pattern audit
