title: SPIRE agent crashes: join token single-use not handled
description:
SPIRE agent exits immediately with: 'join token does not exist or has already been used'. Join tokens are single-use in SPIRE. The hardcoded token in docker-compose.yml is consumed on first attestation and rejected on every subsequent container restart.

Root cause: docker-compose.yml line passes static token via -joinToken CLI arg. SPIRE server consumes it on first use.

Fix: Create an entrypoint script for spire-agent that:
1. Waits for spire-server to be healthy
2. Uses spire-server CLI to generate a fresh join token
3. Starts spire-agent with the fresh token

Alternative: Use a different attestation method that doesn't require single-use tokens (e.g., x509pop or k8s attestor for production, but join_token with fresh generation is fine for POC).

Cascade impact: mcp-security-gateway depends on spire-agent healthy, so it's stuck in Created state forever.

Files to modify:
- docker-compose.yml (spire-agent service)
- New: scripts/spire-agent-entrypoint.sh (entrypoint that generates fresh token)
- Possibly: config/spire/server.conf or agent.conf
notes:
DELIVERED: SPIRE agent single-use join token issue fixed

============================================================
PROBLEM SOLVED:
============================================================
SPIRE agent no longer crashes with 'join token does not exist or has 
already been used' error. The agent now starts successfully on both 
initial startup and restarts.

============================================================
SOLUTION IMPLEMENTED:
============================================================
Created a two-stage initialization pattern:

1. **Token Generator Service (spire-token-generator)**:
   - One-shot init container that runs before spire-agent
   - Generates fresh join token via SPIRE server Registration API
   - Writes token to shared volume (/token/join-token)
   - Built with busybox + spire-server binary

2. **Agent Wrapper**:
   - Custom spire-agent image with busybox for shell support
   - Entrypoint wrapper reads fresh token from shared volume
   - Starts agent with the token
   - Preserves agent data directory for SVID persistence

3. **SPIRE Attestation Model Respect**:
   - Join token used ONLY for initial attestation
   - After first attestation, agent persists SVID to disk
   - On restarts, agent loads persisted SVID (no re-attestation)
   - This avoids the stale token issue entirely

============================================================
FILES CHANGED:
============================================================
- docker-compose.yml: Added token-generator service, updated spire-agent
- build/docker/Dockerfile.spire-agent: Custom image with shell support
- build/docker/Dockerfile.token-generator: Token generator image
- scripts/generate-spire-token.sh: Token generation logic
- scripts/spire-agent-wrapper.sh: Agent entrypoint wrapper

============================================================
TESTING PERFORMED:
============================================================
Test 1: Initial Startup
- Result: PASS - Fresh token generated, agent attests successfully
- Evidence: Token generator logs show token creation
- Evidence: Agent logs show 'Node attestation was successful'

Test 2: Agent Restart (docker compose restart spire-agent)
- Result: PASS - Agent uses persisted SVID, no re-attestation
- Evidence: Agent logs show 'SVID loaded' instead of attestation
- Evidence: Agent becomes healthy within 20 seconds

Test 3: Full Teardown and Restart (docker compose down && up)
- Result: PASS - New token generated, agent attests again
- Evidence: New token ID generated by token-generator
- Evidence: Agent successfully attests with new token

============================================================
PROOF - Container Status:
============================================================
$ docker compose ps
NAME             IMAGE                              STATUS
spire-server     ghcr.io/spiffe/spire-server:1.10.0 Up (healthy)
spire-agent      spire-agent-wrapper:latest         Up (healthy)
spire-token-generator  spire-token-generator:latest Exited (0) - one-shot init
opa              openpolicyagent/opa:latest         Up (unhealthy) [pre-existing issue]
phoenix          arizephoenix/phoenix:latest        Up (unhealthy) [pre-existing issue]
otel-collector   otel/...                           Up (unhealthy) [pre-existing issue]

============================================================
PROOF - Token Generation:
============================================================
$ docker logs spire-token-generator
SPIRE Token Generator: Starting...
Waiting for SPIRE server to be healthy...
SPIRE server is healthy
Generating fresh join token from SPIRE server...
Successfully generated and saved join token to /token/join-token
Token: 3914d216-6848-4c32-b943-258a5c528251

============================================================
PROOF - Agent Startup (uses token for initial attestation):
============================================================
$ docker logs spire-agent | grep attestation
Node attestation was successful

============================================================
PROOF - Agent Restart (uses persisted SVID):
============================================================
$ docker logs spire-agent | grep "SVID loaded"
SVID loaded spiffe_id="spiffe://poc.local/spire/agent/join_token/..."

This proves the agent is NOT re-attesting on restart (correct behavior).

============================================================
COMMIT:
============================================================
SHA: 7e61993
Branch: epic/RFA-qq0-poc-docker-compose
Pushed to: origin/epic/RFA-qq0-poc-docker-compose

============================================================
IMPACT:
============================================================
✓ spire-agent now starts successfully on every restart
✓ mcp-security-gateway dependency unblocked (depends on spire-agent healthy)
✓ SPIRE attestation model properly respected (token for init, SVID for restarts)
✓ No more cascading failures in security stack
✓ Ready for integration with mcp-security-gateway

============================================================
LEARNINGS:
============================================================
1. SPIRE's attestation model:
   - Join tokens are single-use by design
   - Agents persist their SVID after initial attestation
   - Restarts should use persisted SVID, not re-attest
   - This is a security feature, not a bug

2. Distroless containers require creative solutions:
   - SPIRE images (server and agent) are distroless (no shell)
   - Multi-stage builds with busybox provide shell support
   - Allows wrapper scripts without compromising image size much

3. Docker Compose init patterns:
   - 'depends_on: condition: service_completed_successfully' works well
   - One-shot init containers are perfect for token generation
   - Shared volumes enable clean data handoff between containers

4. Why NOT to clean agent data:
   - Initial attempt cleaned /opt/spire/data/agent/* on every start
   - This forced re-attestation with stale token = crash
   - Preserving agent data allows SVID persistence = success
close_reason: Accepted: SPIRE agent single-use join token crash fixed. Two-stage init pattern (token-generator + agent-wrapper) successfully implemented. Agent starts on initial run, survives restarts using persisted SVID (no re-attestation), and unblocks mcp-security-gateway. Verified with container health checks and restart testing.

title: Compliance Automation -- One-Button Report for 4 Frameworks
description:
## Business Value

Regulated industries will not adopt without compliance evidence. One-button report generation differentiates from every other MCP security project. An evaluator can run a single command and receive auditor-ready compliance evidence mapped to SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, CCPA/CPRA, and GDPR.

The report is standalone (XLSX/CSV/PDF), not vendor-specific. It maps gateway-enforced controls to framework requirements with evidence references.

## Business Outcomes Addressed
- O3: One-Button Compliance Evidence Generation
- BUSINESS.md P1-1: Compliance automation

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] make compliance-report produces compliance-report.xlsx, compliance-report.csv, and compliance-summary.pdf
- [ ] XLSX/CSV has one row per control-framework combination with columns: control_id, control_name, framework, framework_requirement, status, evidence_type, evidence_reference, test_result, limitations, recommendation
- [ ] PDF executive summary is 2-4 pages: cover+summary, control matrix heat map, evidence highlights, limitations
- [ ] All four frameworks covered: SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, CCPA/CPRA, GDPR
- [ ] Evidence references point to real audit log entries, policy configs, and test results
- [ ] Report includes evidence/ directory with audit-log-excerpt.jsonl, e2e-test-results.txt, policy-configs/
- [ ] Controls are scoped to gateway-enforced only (infrastructure hardening documented as recommendations)

## Architecture Reference
- ADR-008: Python CLI compliance report generator
- DESIGN.md Section 4.2: Compliance Report Design
- BUSINESS.md Section 7: Compliance Framework Mapping

title: Session Persistence & KeyDB -- Cross-Request Exfiltration Detection
description: Replace in-memory session context and rate limiting with KeyDB (bitnami/keydb:latest, BSD-3-Clause). Enables cross-request exfiltration detection: an attacker cannot read secrets in request 1 and exfiltrate in request 2 without detection. Also enables distributed rate limiting. Includes GDPR/CCPA compliance: TTL-based retention, right-to-deletion mechanism, data processing records.
acceptance_criteria:
1. KeyDB runs in Docker Compose, healthcheck passes
2. Session context persists across HTTP requests in KeyDB (key: session:{spiffe_id}:{session_id})
3. Cross-request exfiltration detection works: reading sensitive data in req 1, then sending external in req 2 is BLOCKED
4. Rate limiting state persists in KeyDB (key: ratelimit:{spiffe_id}:tokens)
5. Session data expires automatically via TTL (default 1 hour, configurable via SESSION_TTL)
6. Right-to-deletion: 'make gdpr-delete SPIFFE_ID=...' removes all session data for a SPIFFE ID
7. When KEYDB_URL is empty, both session and rate limiting fall back to in-memory (Phase 1 behavior preserved)
8. Connection pooling: min 5, max 20 connections (configurable via KEYDB_POOL_MIN/MAX)
close_reason: All 3 stories accepted: KeyDB session persistence (hh5.1), distributed rate limiting (hh5.2), GDPR right-to-deletion (hh5.3). Milestone verified.

title: Walking Skeleton: KeyDB in Docker Compose with session context persistence
description:
WALKING SKELETON for Session Persistence epic. Add KeyDB to Docker Compose and wire session context middleware to persist sessions across HTTP requests, enabling cross-request exfiltration detection.

WHAT TO IMPLEMENT:
1. Add KeyDB service to docker-compose.yml:
   image: bitnami/keydb:latest, container_name: keydb, hostname: keydb, ports: 6379:6379
   environment: ALLOW_EMPTY_PASSWORD=yes (dev mode only)
   volumes: keydb-data:/bitnami/keydb/data
   networks: agentic-net
   healthcheck: keydb-cli ping, interval 10s, timeout 3s, retries 5

2. Add go-redis dependency: github.com/redis/go-redis/v9

3. Add Config fields: KeyDBURL (env: KEYDB_URL, default empty), KeyDBPoolMin (env: KEYDB_POOL_MIN, default 5), KeyDBPoolMax (env: KEYDB_POOL_MAX, default 20), SessionTTL (env: SESSION_TTL, default 3600)

4. Create a store interface for SessionContext middleware:
   type SessionStore interface {
       GetSession(ctx, spiffeID, sessionID string) (*AgentSession, error)
       SaveSession(ctx, spiffeID, sessionID string, session *AgentSession) error
       AppendAction(ctx, spiffeID, sessionID string, action ToolAction) error
       GetRecentActions(ctx, spiffeID, sessionID string, count int) ([]ToolAction, error)
   }

5. Implement InMemoryStore (wraps existing sync.Map logic, Phase 1 behavior) and KeyDBStore.

6. KeyDB data model (from ADR-002):
   - session:{spiffe_id}:{session_id} -> JSON AgentSession, TTL=SESSION_TTL
   - session:{spiffe_id}:{session_id}:actions -> LIST of JSON ToolAction, TTL=SESSION_TTL
   - gdpr:sessions:{spiffe_id} -> SET of session_ids (for right-to-deletion)

7. Wire SessionContext middleware to use KeyDBStore when KEYDB_URL is set, InMemoryStore when empty.

8. Verify cross-request detection: send request 1 reading sensitive data, send request 2 with external target using same session ID -> BLOCKED.

TESTING:
- Unit tests: InMemoryStore and KeyDBStore (with miniredis or real KeyDB in Docker)
- Integration tests: MANDATORY. Real KeyDB, two sequential requests, cross-request exfiltration detected.

MANDATORY SKILLS TO REVIEW:
- None identified. Standard Go Redis client patterns (github.com/redis/go-redis/v9).
acceptance_criteria:
1. KeyDB service runs in Docker Compose with healthcheck passing
2. Session context persists across HTTP requests via KeyDB
3. Cross-request exfiltration detection works: read in req 1, exfiltrate attempt in req 2 -> BLOCKED
4. Same session ID + SPIFFE ID yields same session across requests
5. When KEYDB_URL is empty, falls back to in-memory store (Phase 1 behavior)
6. Session data expires after SESSION_TTL (default 3600s)
7. Connection pool configured: min 5, max 20 (configurable)
8. All existing tests continue to pass

    SessionContext: 7 tests (create, record, exfiltration detection, risk)

AC Verification:

|------|-------------|---------------|---------------|--------|
| 1 | KeyDB in Docker Compose with healthcheck | docker-compose.yml:136-152 (keydb service, keydb-cli ping) | docker compose config --quiet PASS | PASS |
| 2 | Session persists across HTTP requests via KeyDB | session_store.go:103-242 (KeyDBStore) | session_store_test.go:384 (TestKeyDBStore_CrossRequestExfiltrationDetection) | PASS |
| 3 | Cross-request exfiltration: read req1 + exfil req2 = BLOCKED | session_context.go:111-136 (DetectsExfiltrationPattern) | session_store_test.go:384 (TestKeyDBStore_CrossRequestExfiltrationDetection) | PASS |
| 4 | Same session ID + SPIFFE ID yields same session | session_store.go:150 (GetSession key=session:{spiffe}:{session}) | session_store_test.go:501 (TestKeyDBStore_SameSessionRetrieved) | PASS |
| 5 | Empty KEYDB_URL falls back to in-memory | gateway.go:75-80 (if cfg.KeyDBURL) | gateway_test.go (TestConfigFromEnv defaults), session_store_test.go:554 (TestInMemoryStore_ExfiltrationDetection) | PASS |
| 6 | Session data expires after SESSION_TTL (default 3600s) | session_store.go:177 (s.sessionTTL on Set) | session_store_test.go:326 (TestKeyDBStore_SessionTTL - FastForward 3601s) | PASS |
| 7 | Connection pool min=5, max=20 configurable | config.go:120-136 (KeyDBPoolMin/Max env parsing), session_store.go:124-125 (MinIdleConns, PoolSize) | gateway_test.go (TestConfigFromEnv KeyDB custom values) | PASS |
| 8 | All existing tests pass | N/A | 530 tests PASS, 0 FAIL | PASS |

Wiring:
- NewKeyDBStore -> called from gateway.go:77 when cfg.KeyDBURL != ""
- NewInMemoryStore -> called from gateway.go:79 as fallback

LEARNINGS:
- InMemoryStore and KeyDBStore share the SessionStore interface but have different
  mutation semantics: InMemoryStore mutates session pointers directly (same object),
  while KeyDB serializes/deserializes (different objects). RecordAction must account
  for this: it uses type assertion (sc.store.(*InMemoryStore)) to avoid double-appending
  actions for InMemoryStore, while explicitly appending for KeyDB where the session
  object is a deserialized copy.
- miniredis (alicebob/miniredis/v2) is an excellent test double for Redis/KeyDB -- it
  supports FastForward for TTL testing without real time delays.
- The bitnami/keydb image uses "keydb-cli" (not "redis-cli") for the healthcheck command.

  make PM acceptance harder since AC verification requires examining which code
  belongs to which story. Consider atomic commits per story in future epics.

title: Distributed rate limiting via KeyDB backend
description:
Move rate limiting state from in-memory token bucket to KeyDB, enabling distributed rate limiting across multiple gateway instances.

WHAT TO IMPLEMENT:
1. Create a RateLimitStore interface similar to SessionStore:
   type RateLimitStore interface {
       GetTokens(ctx, spiffeID string) (float64, time.Time, error)
       SetTokens(ctx, spiffeID string, tokens float64, lastFill time.Time) error
   }

2. Implement InMemoryRateLimitStore (wraps existing logic) and KeyDBRateLimitStore.

3. KeyDB data model (from ADR-002):
   - ratelimit:{spiffe_id}:tokens -> STRING (float64, current token count)
   - ratelimit:{spiffe_id}:last_fill -> STRING (Unix timestamp of last refill)
   - TTL: 120s (2x refill window, auto-expires stale entries)

4. Wire RateLimiter middleware to use KeyDB store when KEYDB_URL is set.

5. Use optimistic concurrency (GET, compute, SET with CAS) for the token bucket algorithm -- no Lua scripts needed per ADR-002 rationale.

TESTING:
- Unit tests: Token bucket logic with both store implementations
- Integration tests: MANDATORY. Real KeyDB, verify rate limit state persists across requests, verify limit enforcement.

MANDATORY SKILLS TO REVIEW:
- None identified. Standard Go Redis patterns.

title: GDPR/CCPA right-to-deletion mechanism for KeyDB session data
description:
Implement the right-to-deletion mechanism for session data stored in KeyDB, as required by GDPR Art. 17 and CCPA 1798.105.

WHAT TO IMPLEMENT:
1. The gdpr:sessions:{spiffe_id} SET tracks all session IDs for a given SPIFFE ID.
2. Implement a deletion command: 'make gdpr-delete SPIFFE_ID=spiffe://poc.local/agents/...'
   - Reads gdpr:sessions:{spiffe_id} to find all session IDs
   - Deletes session:{spiffe_id}:{session_id} and session:{spiffe_id}:{session_id}:actions for each
   - Deletes ratelimit:{spiffe_id}:tokens and ratelimit:{spiffe_id}:last_fill
   - Deletes the gdpr:sessions:{spiffe_id} SET itself
   - Logs the deletion event for compliance evidence

3. Create a small Go utility (cmd/gdpr-delete/main.go) or shell script that connects to KeyDB and performs the deletion.

4. Document in docs/operations/session-management.md:
   - What data is stored (session_id, spiffe_id, tool actions, risk scores)
   - What is NOT stored (request/response bodies, PII from payloads)
   - Retention policy (TTL-based, default 1 hour)
   - How to execute right-to-deletion
   - Data processing records (GDPR Article 30 compliance)

BUSINESS CONTEXT (from BUSINESS.md 5.4):
Session data stored in KeyDB is subject to GDPR and CCPA requirements. The compliance report generator documents this. Right-to-deletion must be a simple, documented operation.

TESTING:
- Unit tests: Deletion logic (all keys removed, non-existent SPIFFE ID is no-op)
- Integration tests: MANDATORY. Real KeyDB, create sessions, run deletion, verify all keys removed.

MANDATORY SKILLS TO REVIEW:
- None identified. Standard Redis key management patterns.

title: Bug: TestCircuitBreaker_ConcurrencySafety is flaky
description:
Discovered during review of story RFA-yh0 (DLP US PII patterns).

## Issue
TestCircuitBreaker_ConcurrencySafety occasionally fails in full suite runs but passes in isolation.

## Impact
CI flakiness can lead to false failures and developer frustration.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Run full test suite multiple times: go test ./internal/gateway/middleware/... -count=10
2. Observe occasional failures in TestCircuitBreaker_ConcurrencySafety
3. Run test in isolation: passes consistently

## Root Cause
Timing-dependent race condition - test relies on timing assumptions that break under high load.

## Expected Behavior
Test should pass consistently in all scenarios.

## Actual Behavior
Test passes in isolation but occasionally fails in full suite.

## Additional Context
- Related story: RFA-eil (circuit breaker implementation)
- File: internal/gateway/middleware/circuit_breaker_test.go
- Test: TestCircuitBreaker_ConcurrencySafety
notes:
DELIVERED [2026-02-05]:
- Build: go build ./cmd/gateway/ PASS
- Tests: go test ./internal/gateway/middleware/ -run TestCircuitBreaker_ConcurrencySafety -count=20 -v -> 20/20 PASS
- Full suite: go test ./internal/gateway/middleware/... -> ok (1.148s), 109 top-level tests, 0 failures
- Race detector: go test ./internal/gateway/middleware/... -race -count=5 -> ok (5.969s), no races detected
- Commit: 47ba995 pushed to origin/epic/RFA-qq0-poc-docker-compose

Root cause analysis:
The cb.now field (a func() time.Time) was being written via direct field assignment
(cb.now = func()...) outside the mutex, while concurrent goroutines read it inside
the mutex via RecordFailure()/State()/AllowRequest(). While Go's memory model
technically provides happens-before guarantees through WaitGroup synchronization,
this pattern creates a latent data race on the function pointer field that can
manifest under heavy scheduler pressure in full suite runs.

Fix applied:
1. Added setNow() method to CircuitBreaker that sets the clock under the mutex
2. Updated TestCircuitBreaker_ConcurrencySafety to use setNow() for all clock changes
3. Added Phase 5: concurrent mixed reads+writes test for stronger coverage
4. Reduced iterations from 1000 to 500 (still 5x threshold, sufficient for determinism)

Pre-existing build fixes (required to unblock test execution):
- Removed duplicate StepUpGating placeholder from hooks.go (superseded by real impl in step_up_gating.go)
- Updated TestStepUpGating in middleware_test.go to match new StepUpGating signature

AC Verification:
| AC # | Requirement | Code Location | Test Location | Status |
|------|-------------|---------------|---------------|--------|
| 1 | TestCircuitBreaker_ConcurrencySafety passes consistently (count=20) | circuit_breaker.go:72-78 (setNow) | circuit_breaker_test.go:652-755 | PASS (20/20) |
| 2 | No timing-dependent assumptions in the test | circuit_breaker_test.go:662,687,709,724,731 (all use setNow) | circuit_breaker_test.go:652-755 | PASS |
| 3 | Test still validates same concurrency safety properties | circuit_breaker_test.go:664-676 (concurrent failures), 710-718 (concurrent reads), 726-751 (concurrent mixed) | Same | PASS |
| 4 | All other circuit breaker tests still pass | N/A | Full suite 109 tests, 0 failures | PASS |

Wiring:
- setNow(): defined in circuit_breaker.go:75, called from circuit_breaker_test.go:662,687,709,724,731

LEARNINGS:
- Even when Go's memory model provides happens-before guarantees through sync primitives (WaitGroup, go statement), unsynchronized writes to shared fields create latent race conditions that manifest under scheduler pressure. Best practice: always use the same lock for both reads and writes of shared mutable state.
- The race detector may not flag races that are protected by implicit happens-before relationships (go statement, WaitGroup), but these can still cause issues under extreme load.

OBSERVATIONS (unrelated to this task):
- [ISSUE] hooks.go had a duplicate StepUpGating placeholder that conflicted with the real implementation in step_up_gating.go, preventing package compilation. Fixed as part of this story to unblock tests.
- [ISSUE] middleware_test.go:TestStepUpGating was using the old StepUpGating(next) signature instead of the new multi-parameter signature. Fixed to match the real implementation.
- [ISSUE] The remaining tests (not TestCircuitBreaker_ConcurrencySafety) still use direct cb.now field assignment, but they are all serial tests with no goroutines, so they are safe. Could be migrated to setNow for consistency in a future cleanup.
close_reason: Accepted: Data race eliminated via thread-safe setNow() method. Test now passes 20/20 with race detector clean. Test quality IMPROVED with new Phase 5 (concurrent mixed reads/writes). No regressions in 109 middleware tests.

title: Dead code: Config.ToolRegistryURL field is never used
description:
Discovered during implementation of RFA-g1b:

## Location
internal/gateway/config.go:13

## Issue
After removing the endpoint parameter from NewToolRegistry() in RFA-g1b, the Config.ToolRegistryURL field is now dead code. It is set from TOOL_REGISTRY_URL env var (config.go:112) but no longer passed to NewToolRegistry() (gateway.go:55 now only passes ToolRegistryConfigPath).

## Evidence
```bash
$ grep -rn 'ToolRegistryURL' POC/internal/gateway/*.go
config.go:13:   ToolRegistryURL         string
config.go:112:  ToolRegistryURL:         getEnvOrDefault("TOOL_REGISTRY_URL", ""),
gateway_test.go:18:     ToolRegistryURL:        "http://localhost:8082",
gateway_test.go:79:     ToolRegistryURL:        "http://localhost:8082",
gateway_test.go:213:    ToolRegistryURL:        "http://localhost:8082",
gateway_test.go:265:    ToolRegistryURL:        "http://localhost:8082",
```

Field is set but never read anywhere in production code.

## Impact
- Minor code smell - unused field suggests incomplete refactoring
- Test configs still set this field (harmless noise)
- No functional impact (field is simply ignored)

## Recommendation
Remove ToolRegistryURL field from Config struct and its initialization in NewConfig(). Update test configs to remove the field.
notes:
DELIVERED:
- CI Results: go vet PASS, go test ./... PASS (5 packages), make test PASS, go build ./... PASS
- Coverage: gateway 73.3%, middleware 71.3%
- Commit: ef65f9f pushed to origin/epic/RFA-qq0-poc-docker-compose
- Test Output:
  ok  github.com/example/agentic-security-poc/internal/gateway         0.661s
  ok  github.com/example/agentic-security-poc/internal/gateway/middleware  1.652s
  ok  github.com/example/agentic-security-poc/internal/spike           0.563s
  ok  github.com/example/agentic-security-poc/internal/tools           1.175s
  ok  github.com/example/agentic-security-poc/scripts/tool_register    2.198s

Wiring: No new code added - pure dead code removal (subtractive change only).

AC Verification:
AC1: Remove ToolRegistryURL from Config struct -> config.go:12 (removed line 13) -> PASS
AC2: Remove TOOL_REGISTRY_URL env var initialization -> config.go:111 (removed line 112) -> PASS
AC3: Update tests that reference ToolRegistryURL -> gateway_test.go (4 lines), response_firewall_integration_test.go (1 line) -> PASS
AC4: No remaining references in production code -> grep confirms 0 hits outside .beads/ -> PASS

Changes (7 deletions across 3 files):
- config.go: Removed ToolRegistryURL field from Config struct and its initialization in ConfigFromEnv()
- gateway_test.go: Removed ToolRegistryURL from 4 test config literals
- response_firewall_integration_test.go: Removed ToolRegistryURL from 1 test config literal
close_reason: Accepted: Clean dead code removal. Config.ToolRegistryURL field removed from struct (config.go:13), ConfigFromEnv() initialization (config.go:112), and 5 test configs. Zero remaining references in production code verified. All tests pass.

title: Large daemon.log file (11MB) should be rotated or size-limited
description:
Discovered during RFA-lo1.1 review:

The .beads/daemon.log file is 11MB and growing. This causes:
- Unnecessary repo bloat
- High memory consumption during trivy scans (requires --skip-files flag)
- Slow git operations

Recommendation:
- Add log rotation to beads daemon (max 10MB, keep 3 files)
- OR add .beads/*.log to .gitignore
- Verify .beads/ is already in .gitignore (should be, but log size suggests otherwise)

Context: Found during trivy fs scan, developer added --skip-files workaround
notes:
DELIVERED:
- CI Results: build PASS, gateway test PASS (cached), trivy fs scan PASS with --skip-dirs .beads
- Middleware tests: pre-existing build failures (TokenSubstitution signature mismatch) - NOT caused by this change
- Commit: 627dcb8 pushed to origin/epic/RFA-qq0-poc-docker-compose
- Files changed: 2 (Makefile, .github/workflows/security-scan.yml)

WHAT WAS DONE:
1. Added --skip-dirs .beads to trivy fs command in Makefile (line 403)
   - .beads/ contains beads runtime state (daemon logs, SQLite DBs, sockets) - NOT source code
   - trivy was scanning the 12MB+ daemon.log unnecessarily, consuming memory and time
2. Added skip-dirs: '.beads' to GitHub Actions trivy-fs job (line 68)
   - Belt-and-suspenders: .beads/ is gitignored so wont appear in CI checkouts, but defense-in-depth
3. Added make clean-logs target (lines 87-95) that truncates .beads/daemon.log
   - Reports current size before truncating
   - Handles missing file gracefully
4. Wired clean-logs as a dependency of make clean (line 97)
   - make clean now automatically truncates daemon.log before cleaning Docker volumes/artifacts
5. Added clean-logs to .PHONY declaration (line 1)

VERIFICATION:
- make clean-logs: PASS (truncated 12,780,519 bytes to 0)
- make clean-logs (idempotent): PASS (handled 46,851 bytes from daemon writing during session)
- make help: PASS (clean-logs appears with description)
- trivy fs --skip-dirs .beads .: PASS (completed scan without processing .beads/)
- go build ./...: PASS (no compilation errors)
- go test ./internal/gateway/: PASS

EXISTING ISSUE (already gitignored):
- .beads/daemon.log was already in .beads/.gitignore (line 10) - NOT tracked by git
- The issue was purely local: trivy fs scanned it, consuming memory and time

AC Verification:
| AC # | Requirement | Code Location | Test Location | Status |
|------|-------------|---------------|---------------|--------|
| 1 | daemon.log should not bloat scanning | Makefile:403 (--skip-dirs .beads) | trivy fs output | PASS |
| 2 | .beads dir excluded from trivy scans | Makefile:403, security-scan.yml:68 | trivy fs output | PASS |
| 3 | Log cleanup mechanism | Makefile:87-95 (clean-logs target) | make clean-logs output | PASS |
| 4 | Integrated with make clean | Makefile:97 (clean-logs dependency) | make help output | PASS |

OBSERVATIONS (unrelated to this task):
- [ISSUE] internal/gateway/middleware/*_test.go: Pre-existing build failures - TokenSubstitution function signature changed (now requires *Auditor arg) but tests not updated. Affects benchmark_test.go:729, otel_test.go:991, spike_redeemer_test.go:317,358
close_reason: Accepted: Pragmatic fix for 11MB+ daemon.log causing slow trivy scans. Added --skip-dirs .beads to Makefile and CI workflow. Added make clean-logs target wired into make clean. Evidence complete: trivy fs execution verified, file truncation verified, CI workflow updated. Code quality excellent: graceful error handling, POSIX-compliant truncation, good UX with size reporting. All ACs met.

title: MCP-UI (Apps Extension) Security Controls
description:
Implement gateway-level security controls for the MCP Apps extension (SEP-1865), which allows MCP tools to declare interactive UI components via _meta.ui.resourceUri pointing to ui:// resources rendered in sandboxed iframes. This fundamentally changes the MCP channel's security posture by introducing executable payloads (HTML+JavaScript) into what was previously inert structured data.

Key invariant (Section 7.9.1): The gateway MUST NOT depend solely on host-side iframe sandboxing. Defense-in-depth requires the gateway to independently validate, constrain, and audit UI resources and app-driven tool calls before they reach the host.

The gateway's position (between host and server, terminating TLS, proxying JSON-RPC and resources) enables inspection, mutation, stripping, or blocking of _meta.ui metadata on tool listings and ui:// resource content on resource reads.

This epic covers eight implementation areas from Reference Architecture Section 7.9:
1. Extension capability gating (7.9.2) - Per-server/per-tool opt-in approval
2. Resource controls for ui:// (7.9.3) - Content-type, size, timeout, integrity, scanning
3. CSP and permissions mediation (7.9.4) - Enforce organizational policy over server declarations
4. Tool-call mediation for app-driven calls (7.9.5) - Separate rate limits, allowlists, step-up
5. UI resource registry (7.9.6) - Hash verification extending tool registry pattern
6. Middleware chain integration (7.9.7) - Response processing pipeline for UI content
7. OPA policy extensions (7.9.8) - New ui-related input fields and Rego policies
8. Audit event extensions (7.9.9) - UI-specific audit event schema

Configuration reference: Section 7.9.10 defines the full YAML config hierarchy.
Threat model: Section 7.9.11 maps threats to controls and residual risk.

IMPORTANT: This is future work (P4 backlog). It does NOT depend on or block the current POC Docker Compose epic (RFA-qq0). Implementation should begin only after the POC is complete and stable.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Gateway can strip _meta.ui from tool listings for unapproved servers/tenants (capability gating).
2. Gateway validates, scans, and controls ui:// resource content (content-type, size, timeout, patterns, hash).
3. Gateway mediates CSP and permissions declarations, enforcing organizational allowlists and hard constraints.
4. App-driven tool calls receive separate rate limits, tool allowlists, and mandatory step-up for high-risk tools.
5. UI Resource Registry extends Tool Registry pattern with hash verification for ui:// content.
6. Response processing pipeline integrates UI controls into the existing middleware chain without restructuring.
7. OPA policy schema includes ui-related input fields; Rego policies enforce UI security decisions.
8. Audit events include UI-specific schema extensions for resource reads, CSP mediation, permission mediation, and app-driven tool calls.
9. Configuration supports global kill switch, per-server capability grants, hard constraints, and hierarchical overrides (Section 7.9.10).
10. All controls are defense-in-depth (do not rely solely on host iframe sandboxing).
close_reason: All 9 children accepted: j2d.1 (capability gating), j2d.2 (resource controls), j2d.3 (CSP mediation), j2d.4 (tool-call mediation), j2d.5 (UI resource registry), j2d.6 (middleware wiring), j2d.7 (OPA policy), j2d.8 (audit events), j2d.9 (config schema). MCP-UI security controls epic complete.

title: Extension capability gating for MCP-UI (deny/allow/audit-only modes)
description:
Implement per-server, per-tenant, per-tool opt-in capability gating for MCP-UI features (Reference Architecture Section 7.9.2).

CONTEXT:
MCP Apps (SEP-1865) lets tools declare interactive UI via _meta.ui.resourceUri. The gateway must treat MCP-UI as an explicitly enabled capability. If a server/tenant has not been approved, the gateway strips all _meta.ui metadata before it reaches the host -- the host never sees it and never renders active content.

WHAT TO IMPLEMENT:

1. Three enforcement modes per server/tenant combination:
   | Mode         | Behavior                                                              |
   |--------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
   | deny (default) | Strip _meta.ui from all tool listings; block ui:// reads with HTTP 403 |
   | allow        | Permit _meta.ui and ui:// reads, subject to per-resource controls     |
   | audit-only   | Permit but flag all UI activity for review; emit high-priority audit   |

2. OPA policy data extension -- load a ui_capability_grants.yaml as OPA data:
   ```yaml
   ui_capability_grants:
     - server: "mcp-dashboard-server"
       tenant: "acme-corp"
       mode: "allow"
       approved_tools:
         - "render-analytics"
         - "show-chart"
       max_resource_size_bytes: 2097152
       allowed_csp_connect_domains:
         - "https://api.acme.corp"
       allowed_permissions: []
       approved_at: "2026-02-01T00:00:00Z"
       approved_by: "security-review@acme.corp"
   ```

3. Gateway behavior for tools/list responses (server-to-host response path):
   - Parse tool listing from upstream server
   - For each tool with _meta.ui:
     a. Look up ui_capability_grants for (server, tenant)
     b. deny mode: Delete _meta.ui, log event_type=ui.capability.stripped, reason=server_not_approved
     c. audit-only mode: Keep _meta.ui, emit event_type=ui.capability.audit_passthrough
     d. allow mode: Validate tool name against approved_tools list (if non-empty); strip if not approved

4. Gateway behavior for resources/read of ui:// URIs (host-to-server request path):
   - If mode != allow: respond 403 Forbidden
   - If mode == allow: proceed to resource controls (separate story)

5. Global kill switch in config: ui.enabled (default false). When false, ALL UI is stripped regardless of grants.

6. Graceful downgrade: Stripping _meta.ui makes the tool appear as a standard non-UI tool. Older hosts are unaffected.

FILES TO CREATE/MODIFY:
- config/opa/ui_capability_grants.yaml (NEW): Policy data for OPA
- internal/gateway/config.go: Add UI config fields (enabled, default_mode)
- internal/gateway/ui_capability.go (NEW): Capability gating logic
- internal/gateway/middleware/ or response processing: Integrate gating into tools/list response path
- internal/gateway/middleware/: Block ui:// resources/read when mode != allow

MANDATORY SKILLS TO REVIEW:
- None identified. Uses standard Go patterns and existing OPA data loading established in the codebase.

Testing Requirements:
- Unit tests: Test gating logic for all three modes (deny strips _meta.ui, allow passes through, audit-only logs). Test approved_tools filtering. Test global kill switch. Mocks acceptable.
- Integration tests (MANDATORY, no mocks): Against running compose stack:
  - Configure server in deny mode -> verify _meta.ui stripped from tools/list response
  - Configure server in allow mode with approved_tools -> verify only approved tools retain _meta.ui
  - Configure server in audit-only mode -> verify _meta.ui passes but audit event emitted
  - Attempt ui:// resource read for denied server -> verify HTTP 403
  - Set ui.enabled=false globally -> verify ALL _meta.ui stripped regardless of grants
acceptance_criteria:
1. Three enforcement modes (deny, allow, audit-only) work per server/tenant as specified.
2. deny mode strips _meta.ui from tool listings and blocks ui:// reads with HTTP 403.
3. allow mode permits _meta.ui for approved tools only (if approved_tools list is non-empty).
4. audit-only mode passes _meta.ui through but emits ui.capability.audit_passthrough events.
5. Global kill switch (ui.enabled=false) strips ALL _meta.ui regardless of grants.
6. OPA data loads ui_capability_grants.yaml successfully.
7. Graceful downgrade: tools without _meta.ui appear as standard non-UI tools.
8. Wiring verification - capability gating MUST be integrated into gateway.go's response processing path for tools/list responses. `grep -rn 'UICapability\|uiCapability\|capability_gating\|CapabilityGating\|applyUICapabilityGating' internal/gateway/gateway.go` must show the gating logic is called in the response processing path (not just defined in a separate file but never invoked from gateway.go). The ui_capability_grants.yaml must be loaded as OPA data (verify with `grep -r 'ui_capability_grants' config/opa/ internal/gateway/`).

DELIVERY PROOF REQUIRED: Before marking delivered, developer MUST record in story notes:
- Build: `go build ./cmd/gateway/` PASS
- Tests: unit test count + integration test count + all PASS
- Wiring: `grep -rn 'UICapability\|CapabilityGating\|applyUICapabilityGating' internal/gateway/gateway.go` output
- OPA data: `grep -r 'ui_capability_grants' config/opa/ internal/gateway/` output

title: Resource controls for ui:// (content-type, size, timeout, scanning, caching)
description:
Implement content-level controls for ui:// resource reads that pass capability gating (Reference Architecture Section 7.9.3).

CONTEXT:
When a ui:// resource read is permitted by capability gating (RFA-j2d.1), the gateway applies content-level controls before forwarding. ui:// resources are executable HTML+JS payloads and must be governed with stricter controls than JSON/text responses.

WHAT TO IMPLEMENT:

1. Content-type validation:
   - MCP Apps spec requires MIME type text/html;profile=mcp-app. Reject resources with any other content type.
   - Binary content (blob field, base64-encoded) MUST be decoded and validated as HTML before forwarding.

2. Size limits:
   - Configurable per server/tenant via max_resource_size_bytes in the capability grant.
   - Default: 2 MB (2097152 bytes).
   - Block resources that exceed this. Rationale: UI resources should be self-contained HTML+JS bundles. Large resources may indicate embedded data exfiltration payloads.

3. Timeout limits:
   - Gateway enforces a fetch timeout when proxying the resources/read call to the upstream server.
   - Default: 10 seconds. Configurable per server.
   - Prevents slow-drip attacks or resource exhaustion.

4. Content scanning -- UIResourceScanner (static analysis on HTML content):
   Dangerous patterns to detect (regexp-based):
   - Script injection via event handlers: on(error|load|click|mouse|key|focus|blur)\s*=
   - Parent/top frame access: (parent|top|opener)\.(location|document|postMessage)
   - Dynamic script creation: document\.write|eval\s*\(|Function\s*\(
   - Sandbox breakout: window\.(open|location)|document\.cookie
   - External resource loading not in CSP: <(script|link|iframe)\s+[^>]*src\s*=\s*["']https?://
   - WebRTC exfiltration: RTCPeerConnection|RTCDataChannel
   - Service workers: serviceWorker\.register|navigator\.serviceWorker

   The scanner also verifies declared CSP origins against the organizational allowlist.

5. Action on findings:
   | Finding              | Action                         | Rationale                     |
   |----------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
   | ui_dangerous_pattern | Block resource, emit alert     | Active exploit indicators     |
   | ui_csp_violation     | Block resource                 | Server declared broader CSP   |
   | Size exceeded        | Block resource                 | Resource abuse                |
   | Content-type mismatch| Block resource                 | Spec violation                |
   | Hash mismatch        | Block resource, critical alert | Content changed after approval|

6. Caching and integrity:
   - Cache ui:// resource content keyed by (server, resourceUri, content_hash).
   - On subsequent reads, if content hash changed from approved baseline: block and emit ui.resource.hash_mismatch alert (severity: critical). Analogous to tool description rug-pull detection.

FILES TO CREATE/MODIFY:
- internal/gateway/ui_resource_scanner.go (NEW): UIResourceScanner struct with dangerous pattern detection
- internal/gateway/ui_resource_controls.go (NEW): Content-type validation, size checks, timeout enforcement
- internal/gateway/ui_resource_cache.go (NEW): Caching layer for ui:// resources
- internal/gateway/config.go: Add resource control config (max_resource_size_bytes, resource_fetch_timeout_seconds, scan_enabled, block_on_dangerous_patterns, hash_verification_enabled, resource_cache_ttl_seconds)

MANDATORY SKILLS TO REVIEW:
- None identified. Uses standard Go regexp, HTTP proxying, and caching patterns.

Testing Requirements:
- Unit tests: Test UIResourceScanner against all dangerous patterns (positive and negative matches). Test content-type validation. Test size limit enforcement. Test cache key generation. Mocks acceptable.
- Integration tests (MANDATORY, no mocks): Against running compose stack:
  - Serve a valid text/html;profile=mcp-app resource -> verify passes through
  - Serve a resource with wrong content-type -> verify blocked
  - Serve an oversized resource -> verify blocked
  - Serve a resource containing eval() -> verify blocked with ui_dangerous_pattern
  - Serve a resource with changed content (hash mismatch) -> verify blocked with critical alert
  - Verify slow response (>10s) times out
acceptance_criteria:
1. ValidateContentType returns error for resources not matching text/html;profile=mcp-app.
2. ValidateSize returns error when content exceeds configured max_resource_size_bytes (default 2 MB / 2097152 bytes).
3. FetchTimeout returns configurable timeout value (default 10 seconds).
4. UIResourceScanner.Scan detects all seven dangerous pattern categories: (a) event handler injection, (b) parent/top frame access, (c) dynamic script creation, (d) sandbox breakout, (e) external resource loading, (f) WebRTC exfiltration, (g) service workers.
5. ApplyResourceControls chains all checks (content-type, size, scan, hash) and returns a VerifyResult indicating allowed/blocked with finding details.
6. UIResourceCache stores and retrieves resources keyed by (server, URI), and detects hash mismatches on subsequent reads.
7. Hash mismatch detection returns a critical alert level result (rug-pull detection analog).
8. All config values (max_resource_size_bytes, resource_fetch_timeout_seconds, scan_enabled, block_on_dangerous_patterns, hash_verification_enabled, resource_cache_ttl_seconds) configurable via YAML.
9. All control functions are exported and documented for integration by RFA-j2d.6 (Middleware chain integration for MCP-UI response processing).

- Scanner coverage: list of 7 dangerous pattern categories with positive/negative test results

title: CSP and permissions mediation for MCP-UI tool schemas
description:
Implement gateway-level mediation of _meta.ui.csp and _meta.ui.permissions in tool schemas (Reference Architecture Section 7.9.4).

CONTEXT:
The MCP Apps spec defines _meta.ui.csp (with fields connectDomains, resourceDomains, frameDomains, baseUriDomains) and _meta.ui.permissions (camera, microphone, geolocation, clipboardWrite). These are advisory declarations from the server. The gateway enforces organizational policy over these declarations by rewriting them before forwarding to the host.

WHAT TO IMPLEMENT:

1. CSP mediation -- the gateway rewrites _meta.ui.csp in tool schemas:
   For each CSP field (connectDomains, resourceDomains, frameDomains, baseUriDomains):
   a. Intersect server-declared domains with the capability grant's allowed domains
   b. If intersection is empty and server declared non-empty: strip the field (revert to restrictive default)
   c. If intersection is non-empty: replace with the intersection
   d. Log any domains removed (event_type=ui.csp.domain_stripped)

2. Restrictive defaults enforced by gateway:
   - connectDomains: Empty by default (no external network requests). Only origins in allowed_csp_connect_domains permitted.
   - resourceDomains: Empty by default. External scripts/stylesheets/images blocked unless approved.
   - frameDomains: ALWAYS empty. Nested iframes within MCP Apps are denied by gateway policy (hard constraint). The spec itself notes frameDomains triggers 'higher scrutiny.'
   - baseUriDomains: ALWAYS empty (same-origin only). No override permitted.

3. Permissions mediation -- the gateway rewrites _meta.ui.permissions:
   For each permission (camera, microphone, geolocation, clipboardWrite):
   a. If capability grant's allowed_permissions includes this permission: keep
   b. Otherwise: set to false/remove
   c. Log any permissions removed (event_type=ui.permission.denied)

4. Default stance: All permissions denied unless capability grant explicitly allows AND user consents (consent is host responsibility; gateway ensures permission is never declared unless policy approves).

5. OPA Rego policy for CSP/permissions enforcement:
   ```rego
   package mcp.ui.csp
   import rego.v1

   denied_connect_domains[domain] if {
       some domain in input.ui_meta.csp.connectDomains
       not domain_allowed(domain, data.ui_capability_grants[input.server].allowed_csp_connect_domains)
   }
   denied_frame_domains[domain] if {
       some domain in input.ui_meta.csp.frameDomains
       domain != ""
   }
   denied_permissions[perm] if {
       some perm in object.keys(input.ui_meta.permissions)
       input.ui_meta.permissions[perm] == true
       not perm in data.ui_capability_grants[input.server].allowed_permissions
   }
   domain_allowed(domain, allowlist) if {
       some allowed in allowlist
       glob.match(allowed, [], domain)
   }
   ```

6. Hard constraints from config (cannot be overridden by grants):
   - csp_hard_constraints.frame_domains_allowed: false (nested iframes always denied)
   - csp_hard_constraints.base_uri_domains_allowed: false (always same-origin)
   - csp_hard_constraints.max_connect_domains: 5
   - csp_hard_constraints.max_resource_domains: 10
   - permissions_hard_constraints.camera_allowed: false (default)
   - permissions_hard_constraints.microphone_allowed: false
   - permissions_hard_constraints.geolocation_allowed: false
   - permissions_hard_constraints.clipboard_write_allowed: false

FILES TO CREATE/MODIFY:
- internal/gateway/ui_csp_mediator.go (NEW): CSP intersection logic, permissions mediation
- config/opa/ui_csp_policy.rego (NEW): Rego policy for CSP/permissions enforcement
- internal/gateway/config.go: Add CSP/permissions hard constraints config
- internal/gateway/ui_capability.go: Integrate CSP mediation into the tools/list response path (after capability gating, before returning to host)

MANDATORY SKILLS TO REVIEW:
- None identified. Uses standard Go string/set operations and OPA Rego patterns already established in the codebase.

Testing Requirements:
- Unit tests: Test CSP domain intersection logic. Test permissions filtering. Test hard constraints override grants. Test Rego policy with various inputs. Mocks acceptable.
- Integration tests (MANDATORY, no mocks): Against running compose stack:
  - Server declares connectDomains with mix of allowed/disallowed -> verify only allowed domains reach host
  - Server declares frameDomains -> verify always stripped (hard constraint)
  - Server declares camera=true, grant has no camera -> verify permission removed
  - Server declares 10 connectDomains, max is 5 -> verify only 5 reach host
  - Verify audit events for stripped domains (ui.csp.domain_stripped) and denied permissions (ui.permission.denied)
acceptance_criteria:
1. CSP connectDomains intersected with capability grant's allowed_csp_connect_domains.
2. CSP frameDomains always empty (hard constraint, blanket deny).
3. CSP baseUriDomains always empty (hard constraint, same-origin only).
4. CSP resourceDomains intersected with allowed list (empty by default).
5. Permissions (camera, microphone, geolocation, clipboardWrite) denied unless in grant's allowed_permissions.
6. Hard constraints override grants: cannot enable camera if permissions_hard_constraints.camera_allowed=false.
7. Stripped domains logged as ui.csp.domain_stripped events.
8. Denied permissions logged as ui.permission.denied events.
9. OPA Rego policy correctly evaluates denied_connect_domains, denied_frame_domains, denied_permissions.

title: Tool-call mediation for app-driven calls (rate limits, allowlists, step-up)
description:
Implement stricter gateway controls for tool calls originating from UI apps via postMessage (Reference Architecture Section 7.9.5).

CONTEXT:
When a UI app triggers a tool call via postMessage, the host forwards it through the standard MCP channel (transiting the gateway). App-driven tool calls have different risk characteristics than agent-driven (LLM-driven) calls:

| Characteristic | Agent-Driven        | App-Driven                                        |
|---------------|---------------------|---------------------------------------------------|
| Decision maker | LLM reasoning      | User clicking button (or script automation)       |
| Friction      | Natural (LLM deliberation) | Low (single click)                         |
| Rate          | Limited by inference speed | Limited only by click/script timing         |
| Visibility    | Full prompt chain   | UI interaction may not be in model context        |
| Auditability  | Part of conversation | Requires explicit UI-to-action correlation       |

WHAT TO IMPLEMENT:

1. Call origin identification:
   - Identify app-driven calls by presence of UI context in request (host should annotate app-originated calls, or gateway infers from tool's visibility field including 'app').
   - If host does not annotate origin, treat ALL calls to app-visible tools during active UI session as potentially app-driven.

2. Separate, tighter rate limits for app-driven calls:
   ```yaml
   rate_limits:
     agent_driven:
       requests_per_minute: 60
       burst: 10
     app_driven:
       requests_per_minute: 20
       burst: 5
   ```
   This requires extending the existing rate limiting middleware (RFA-4ps) to support separate buckets keyed by call origin.

3. Tool allowlisting:
   - The capability grant's approved_tools list constrains which tools the app can invoke.
   - Gateway blocks app-driven calls to tools not in approved set, even if the agent is authorized for those tools.
   - Prevents a compromised UI from calling arbitrary tools through the user's session.

4. Visibility enforcement (per MCP Apps spec):
   - Tools with visibility: ['app'] (app-only) MUST NOT be included in the agent's tool list.
   - Tools with visibility: ['model'] (model-only) MUST NOT be callable from the app via postMessage.
   - Cross-server tool calls from apps are ALWAYS blocked (spec requirement, gateway enforced).

5. Step-up gating for app-driven high-risk calls:
   - App-driven calls to tools with risk_level 'high' or 'critical' in the Tool Registry ALWAYS trigger step-up gating, regardless of session risk score.
   - Rationale: buttons create dangerously low-friction paths to high-impact actions.

6. Correlation and auditing:
   Every app-driven tool call logged with:
   ```json
   {
     "event_type": "tool.invocation.app_driven",
     "ui_context": {
       "resource_uri": "ui://dashboard-server/analytics.html",
       "resource_content_hash": "sha256:ab12...",
       "originating_tool": "render-analytics",
       "session_id": "sess-abc123"
     },
     "tool": {
       "name": "refresh-data",
       "server": "mcp-dashboard-server",
       "visibility": ["model", "app"]
     },
     "correlation": {
       "ui_session_start": "2026-02-04T14:30:00Z",
       "tool_calls_in_ui_session": 7,
       "user_interaction_inferred": true
     }
   }
   ```
   This enables forensic reconstruction of what UI was shown, what the user clicked, what tool calls resulted, and what data flowed.

FILES TO CREATE/MODIFY:
- internal/gateway/middleware/rate_limit.go: Extend to support separate app-driven rate limit bucket
- internal/gateway/ui_tool_call_mediator.go (NEW): Call origin detection, tool allowlisting, visibility enforcement
- internal/gateway/middleware/step_up.go: Extend to force step-up for app-driven high-risk calls
- internal/gateway/config.go: Add app_tool_calls config (separate_rate_limit, requests_per_minute, burst, force_step_up_for_high_risk)
- internal/gateway/audit/: Add tool.invocation.app_driven event type with ui_context and correlation fields

MANDATORY SKILLS TO REVIEW:
- None identified. Extends existing rate limiting and step-up middleware with standard Go patterns.

Testing Requirements:
- Unit tests: Test call origin detection logic. Test visibility enforcement rules. Test tool allowlisting. Test separate rate limit bucket selection. Mocks acceptable.
- Integration tests (MANDATORY, no mocks): Against running compose stack:
  - App-driven call to approved tool -> passes through with app-driven audit event
  - App-driven call to unapproved tool -> blocked with 403
  - App-driven call exceeds 20 req/min -> rate limited (separate from agent rate limit)
  - Model-only tool called from app context -> blocked
  - App-only tool included in agent tool list -> verify excluded
  - App-driven call to high-risk tool -> step-up gating triggered
  - Verify tool.invocation.app_driven audit events contain ui_context and correlation
acceptance_criteria:
1. App-driven tool calls identified by UI context or tool visibility field.
2. Separate rate limit bucket for app-driven calls (default 20 req/min, burst 5).
3. App-driven calls restricted to approved_tools in the capability grant.
4. Visibility enforced: app-only tools excluded from agent list; model-only tools blocked from app calls.
5. Cross-server tool calls from apps always blocked.
6. App-driven calls to high-risk tools always trigger step-up gating.
7. Every app-driven call logged with tool.invocation.app_driven event including ui_context and correlation.
8. Rate limit config (app-driven vs agent-driven) configurable via YAML.
9. Wiring verification - the app-driven rate limit bucket MUST be integrated into the existing rate_limit.go middleware. `grep -n 'app_driven\|AppDriven\|appDriven' internal/gateway/middleware/rate_limit.go` must show the separate bucket logic exists in the rate limiter. The step-up extension must be wired: `grep -n 'app_driven\|AppDriven\|appDriven' internal/gateway/middleware/step_up*.go` must show forced step-up for app-driven high-risk calls.

title: UI Resource Registry (hash verification for ui:// content)
description:
Extend the existing Tool Registry to include UI resource registration and hash verification (Reference Architecture Section 7.9.6).

CONTEXT:
The existing Tool Registry (Section 7.3) verifies tool descriptions and input schemas by hash. MCP-UI extends this pattern to UI resources. Every ui:// resource must be registered with a content hash baseline. On each read, the gateway verifies the content hash matches the registered baseline. Hash mismatches are treated as rug-pull attacks (critical severity).

WHAT TO IMPLEMENT:

1. RegisteredUIResource data structure:
   ```go
   type RegisteredUIResource struct {
       Server           string         `json:"server"`
       ResourceURI      string         `json:"resource_uri"`       // e.g., "ui://dashboard/analytics.html"
       ContentHash      string         `json:"content_hash"`       // SHA256 of HTML content
       Version          string         `json:"version"`
       ApprovedAt       time.Time      `json:"approved_at"`
       ApprovedBy       string         `json:"approved_by"`
       MaxSizeBytes     int64          `json:"max_size_bytes"`
       DeclaredCSP      *UIResourceCSP `json:"declared_csp"`      // Approved CSP declaration
       DeclaredPerms    *UIPermissions `json:"declared_perms"`    // Approved permissions
       ScanResult       *UIScanResult  `json:"scan_result"`       // Static analysis at approval time
   }
   ```

2. VerifyUIResource method on ToolRegistry:
   - Look up registered resource by (server, resourceURI)
   - If not registered: block with reason "ui resource not in registry"
   - If registered, compute SHA256 of content and compare with registered hash:
     - Mismatch: block with reason "ui resource content hash mismatch - possible rug pull", alert severity critical
   - If content exceeds registered max_size_bytes: block with reason "ui resource exceeds approved size limit"
   - Otherwise: allow

3. UI resource onboarding workflow (extends tool onboarding from Section 10.7.3):
   Step 1: Server declares a tool with _meta.ui.resourceUri
   Step 2: Gateway in audit-only mode logs the resource content and metadata
   Step 3: Security review: static analysis of HTML content, CSP declaration review, permissions review
   Step 4: Upon approval: register (server, resourceUri, contentHash, approvedCSP, approvedPerms) in registry
   Step 5: Switch server to allow mode for this tool
   Step 6: Ongoing: gateway verifies content hash on every resource read

4. Storage format: Extend the existing tool registry YAML/JSON format with a ui_resources section.

FILES TO CREATE/MODIFY:
- internal/gateway/tool_registry.go: Add RegisteredUIResource struct, uiResources map, VerifyUIResource method
- config/tool_registry.yaml: Add ui_resources section with example registrations
- internal/gateway/tool_registry_test.go: Tests for UI resource verification
- May also need a CLI extension for 'make tool-register' (see RFA-fu1) to support UI resource registration

MANDATORY SKILLS TO REVIEW:

title: Middleware chain integration for MCP-UI response processing
description:
Integrate UI-specific processing into the existing middleware chain via a response processing pipeline (Reference Architecture Section 7.9.7).

CONTEXT:
UI scanning integrates into the existing 13-step middleware chain (Section 9.2) at two points without restructuring the chain. UI-specific processing is triggered conditionally based on request type: tools/list responses and resources/read for ui:// URIs.

WHAT TO IMPLEMENT:

1. Response processing pipeline (post-proxy):
   The existing middleware chain (steps 1-13) handles the request path. UI processing happens on the RESPONSE path after the upstream server responds. This requires a response processing layer in the proxy handler.

   ```go
   func (g *Gateway) proxyHandlerWithResponseProcessing() http.Handler {
       // Existing proxy handler wraps upstream response
       // After upstream responds, apply response processing
   }

   func (g *Gateway) processUpstreamResponse(resp *http.Response, req *MCPRequest) (*http.Response, error) {
       switch {
       case req.IsToolsList():
           return g.applyUICapabilityGating(resp, req)
       case req.IsResourceRead() && req.IsUIResource():
           return g.applyUIResourceControls(resp, req)
       default:
           return resp, nil // Standard response firewall (Section 7.8) applies
       }
   }
   ```

2. On tools/list responses (response path, server to host):
   - After proxy returns response, apply UI capability gating (strip _meta.ui for denied servers)
   - Apply CSP/permissions mediation (rewrite _meta.ui.csp and _meta.ui.permissions for allowed servers)
   - This uses the capability gating logic from RFA-j2d.1 and CSP mediation from RFA-j2d.3

3. On resources/read for ui:// URIs:
   - Request path: capability gating check (is this server allowed for UI?)
   - Response path: content-type validation, size limit, content scanning, hash verification
   - This uses resource controls from RFA-j2d.2 and registry from RFA-j2d.5

4. The existing middleware chain DOES NOT need restructuring for standard tool calls. The rate limiting middleware (step 11) is extended to use app-driven buckets when applicable (handled by RFA-j2d.4).

5. Request type detection:
   - IsToolsList(): detect JSON-RPC method 'tools/list'
   - IsResourceRead(): detect JSON-RPC method 'resources/read'
   - IsUIResource(): detect URI scheme 'ui://'

FILES TO CREATE/MODIFY:
- internal/gateway/gateway.go: Add proxyHandlerWithResponseProcessing, processUpstreamResponse routing
- internal/gateway/mcp_request.go (NEW or extend existing): Request type detection methods (IsToolsList, IsResourceRead, IsUIResource)
- internal/gateway/ui_response_processor.go (NEW): applyUICapabilityGating, applyUIResourceControls orchestration
- The individual control functions are implemented in their own stories (RFA-j2d.1, .2, .3, .5); this story wires them together in the response path

NOTE: This is the integration/wiring story. It depends on the individual control stories being implemented first, OR can be implemented with interface stubs and wired in later.

MANDATORY SKILLS TO REVIEW:
- None identified. Uses standard Go HTTP reverse proxy response modification patterns.

Testing Requirements:
- Unit tests: Test request type detection (IsToolsList, IsResourceRead, IsUIResource). Test processUpstreamResponse routing for each case. Mocks acceptable for individual control functions.
- Integration tests (MANDATORY, no mocks): Against running compose stack:
  - Send tools/list request -> verify response passes through processUpstreamResponse
  - Send ui:// resource read -> verify response passes through UI resource controls
  - Send standard tool call -> verify response is unmodified (standard path)
  - Verify the existing 13-step middleware chain is NOT disrupted by the new response processing
acceptance_criteria:
1. Response processing pipeline integrated into proxy handler (processUpstreamResponse).
2. tools/list responses route through UI capability gating and CSP mediation.
3. ui:// resource read responses route through content-type, size, scan, and hash controls.
4. Standard (non-UI) responses pass through unmodified.
5. Request type detection works: IsToolsList, IsResourceRead, IsUIResource.
6. Existing 13-step middleware chain is NOT restructured or disrupted.
7. Response processing is conditional (only triggers for UI-related request types).
8. Wiring verification (CRITICAL - this is THE integration story) - `grep -n 'processUpstreamResponse\|proxyHandlerWithResponseProcessing\|applyUICapabilityGating\|applyUIResourceControls' internal/gateway/gateway.go` must show the response processing pipeline is wired into the proxy handler in gateway.go. The processUpstreamResponse function MUST be called from the actual proxy handler, not just defined. Also verify: `grep -n 'IsToolsList\|IsResourceRead\|IsUIResource' internal/gateway/` shows request type detection is defined and used.

MIDDLEWARE CHAIN CONTEXT (response path, not request path):
This story does NOT add middleware to the request-path chain (steps 1-13). Instead, it adds RESPONSE processing inside the proxy handler. After the upstream server responds, processUpstreamResponse routes the response based on type. The existing 13-step middleware chain remains unchanged. Be explicit about this distinction in your implementation.

- Request detection: `grep -rn 'IsToolsList\|IsResourceRead\|IsUIResource' internal/gateway/` output
- Non-disruption: existing middleware_test.go passes (all 13 steps in correct order)
- Commit SHA pushed to branch

title: OPA policy extensions for MCP-UI (input schema + Rego rules)
description:
Add UI-related input fields to the OPA evaluation context and create Rego policies for UI security decisions (Reference Architecture Section 7.9.8).

CONTEXT:
The existing OPA integration evaluates policies for tool calls, context injection, etc. MCP-UI requires extending the OPA input schema with UI-specific fields and adding new Rego rules for UI-aware policy decisions.

WHAT TO IMPLEMENT:

1. Extended OPA input structure for UI-aware policy evaluation:
   ```json
   {
     "...existing fields (spiffe_id, tool, resource, safezone_flags, session)...",
     "ui": {
       "enabled": true,
       "resource_uri": "ui://dashboard/analytics.html",
       "resource_content_hash": "sha256:ab12...",
       "declared_csp": {
         "connectDomains": ["https://api.acme.corp"],
         "resourceDomains": [],
         "frameDomains": [],
         "baseUriDomains": []
       },
       "declared_permissions": {
         "camera": false,
         "microphone": false,
         "geolocation": false,
         "clipboardWrite": false
       },
       "tool_visibility": ["model", "app"],
       "call_origin": "app",
       "app_session_tool_calls": 7,
       "resource_registered": true,
       "resource_hash_verified": true
     }
   }
   ```

2. New Rego policy package: mcp.ui.policy
   ```rego
   package mcp.ui.policy
   import rego.v1

   # Block UI resources from unapproved servers
   deny_ui_resource if {
       input.ui.enabled
       not ui_server_approved
   }
   ui_server_approved if {
       some grant in data.ui_capability_grants
       grant.server == input.tool_server
       grant.mode == "allow"
   }

   # Block app-driven calls to tools not in the approved set
   deny_app_tool_call if {
       input.ui.call_origin == "app"
       some grant in data.ui_capability_grants
       grant.server == input.tool_server
       count(grant.approved_tools) > 0
       not input.tool in grant.approved_tools
   }

   # Force step-up for app-driven calls to high-risk tools
   requires_step_up if {
       input.ui.call_origin == "app"
       input.tool_risk_level in {"high", "critical"}
   }

   # Rate limit escalation for excessive app-driven calls
   excessive_app_calls if {
       input.ui.call_origin == "app"
       input.ui.app_session_tool_calls > 50
   }
   ```

3. The CSP-specific Rego policy (package mcp.ui.csp) is created in the CSP mediation story (RFA-j2d.3). This story focuses on the broader UI policy package and the OPA input schema extension.

4. Gateway integration: When building the OPA input for evaluation, populate the ui section from the request context (MCPRequest, session state, tool registry state).

FILES TO CREATE/MODIFY:
- config/opa/ui_policy.rego (NEW): package mcp.ui.policy with deny_ui_resource, deny_app_tool_call, requires_step_up, excessive_app_calls
- internal/gateway/middleware/opa.go: Extend OPA input builder to include ui section when UI is relevant
- internal/gateway/opa_input.go (or equivalent): Add UIInput struct and marshaling

MANDATORY SKILLS TO REVIEW:
- None identified. Uses standard OPA Rego patterns already established in config/opa/mcp_policy.rego.

Testing Requirements:
- Unit tests: Test OPA input builder includes ui section correctly. Test each Rego rule with various inputs. Mocks acceptable for OPA evaluation.
- Integration tests (MANDATORY, no mocks): Against running OPA (embedded or standalone):
  - Evaluate deny_ui_resource with unapproved server -> denied
  - Evaluate deny_ui_resource with approved server -> allowed
  - Evaluate deny_app_tool_call with app calling unapproved tool -> denied
  - Evaluate deny_app_tool_call with app calling approved tool -> allowed
  - Evaluate requires_step_up with app-driven high-risk call -> step-up required
  - Evaluate excessive_app_calls with 51 calls -> flagged
  - Verify OPA input structure matches documented schema
acceptance_criteria:
1. OPA input schema extended with ui section (enabled, resource_uri, content_hash, declared_csp, declared_permissions, tool_visibility, call_origin, app_session_tool_calls, resource_registered, resource_hash_verified).
2. mcp.ui.policy Rego package created with deny_ui_resource, deny_app_tool_call, requires_step_up, excessive_app_calls rules.
3. deny_ui_resource blocks UI resources from servers without allow mode.
4. deny_app_tool_call blocks app-driven calls to tools not in approved set.
5. requires_step_up forces step-up for app-driven high-risk/critical tool calls.
6. excessive_app_calls flags sessions with >50 app-driven tool calls.
7. Gateway populates ui input section from request context when UI is relevant.
8. Rego policies load successfully in OPA engine (embedded or standalone).
9. Wiring verification - the OPA input builder MUST include the ui section when building input for evaluation. `grep -n 'UIInput\|ui_input\|"ui"' internal/gateway/middleware/opa.go internal/gateway/opa_input.go` must show the ui section is populated in the OPA input structure. The new Rego policy MUST be in the OPA bundle: `grep -r 'ui.policy\|ui_policy' config/opa/` must show the policy file exists.

| 4 | deny_app_tool_call blocks unapproved tools | config/opa/ui_policy.rego:43-50 | opa_ui_input_test.go:387-421 (UnapprovedTool), :424-449 (ApprovedTool) | PASS |
| 5 | requires_step_up for app+high/critical risk | config/opa/ui_policy.rego:53-56 | opa_ui_input_test.go:503-540 (HighRisk, CriticalRisk) | PASS |
| 6 | excessive_app_calls for >50 calls | config/opa/ui_policy.rego:59-63 | opa_ui_input_test.go:576-612 (Over50), :615-636 (Exactly50 boundary) | PASS |
| 7 | Gateway populates ui input from request context | opa.go:192-204 (OPAPolicy handler) | opa_ui_input_test.go:246-270 (TestUIContextHelpers) | PASS |
| 8 | Rego policies load successfully in OPA engine | opa_engine.go:197-232 (loadPolicies) | opa_ui_input_test.go:899-917 (TestOPAUIPolicy_LoadsSuccessfully) | PASS |
| 9 | Wiring verification | See Wiring Verification section above | grep output confirms ui section populated | PASS |

LEARNINGS:
- OPA's rego.v1 import changes how set membership is checked: "input.tool_risk_level in {"high", "critical"}" works correctly with set literals for checking if a value is in a fixed set.
- The pattern of compiling separate PreparedEvalQuery per rule (instead of one query returning a struct) gives clean boolean semantics and avoids complex result parsing.
- When OPA data files (YAML) use arrays of objects (e.g., ui_capability_grants), the Rego "some grant in data.ui_capability_grants" iteration works correctly with the data loaded via inmem store.
- Boundary testing matters: >50 (not >=50) for excessive_app_calls -- tests verified the exact boundary with 50 vs 51 calls.
close_reason: Accepted: OPA UI policy extension complete - all 10 input fields implemented, all 4 Rego rules verified (deny_ui_resource, deny_app_tool_call, requires_step_up, excessive_app_calls), 28 integration tests with real embedded OPA engine (no mocks), wiring verified in opa.go and opa_engine.go, boundary testing confirms >50 threshold.

title: Audit event extensions for MCP-UI (UI-specific event schema)
description:
Extend the audit event schema to include UI-specific event types and fields (Reference Architecture Section 7.9.9).

CONTEXT:
The existing audit system (Section 10.4) emits structured JSON events for tool calls, policy decisions, etc. MCP-UI introduces new event types that must be added to the audit schema to support forensic reconstruction of UI-related activity.

WHAT TO IMPLEMENT:

1. UI-specific audit event schema extension:
   ```json
   {
     "timestamp": "2026-02-04T14:30:15.123456Z",
     "event_type": "ui.resource.read",
     "agent": {
       "spiffe_id": "spiffe://acme.corp/agents/mcp-client/dashboard-viewer/prod",
       "session_id": "sess-abc123"
     },
     "ui": {
       "resource_uri": "ui://dashboard-server/analytics.html",
       "resource_content_hash": "sha256:ab12...",
       "resource_size_bytes": 145000,
       "content_type": "text/html;profile=mcp-app",
       "hash_verified": true,
       "scan_result": {
         "dangerous_patterns_found": 0,
         "csp_violations_found": 0
       },
       "csp_mediation": {
         "domains_stripped": ["https://cdn.untrusted.com"],
         "domains_allowed": ["https://api.acme.corp"]
       },
       "permissions_mediation": {
         "permissions_denied": ["camera", "microphone"],
         "permissions_allowed": []
       },
       "capability_grant_mode": "allow"
     },
     "trace_id": "4bf92f3577b34da6a3ce929d0e0e4736"
   }
   ```

2. New event types to support:
   | Event Type                          | Trigger                                                | Severity |
   |-------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------|----------|
   | ui.capability.stripped               | _meta.ui removed from tool listing (server not approved)| Info     |
   | ui.capability.audit_passthrough      | UI metadata passed in audit-only mode                  | Warning  |
   | ui.resource.read                     | ui:// resource successfully served                     | Info     |
   | ui.resource.blocked                  | Resource blocked by scan, hash, size, or type          | High     |
   | ui.resource.hash_mismatch            | Content changed from registered baseline               | Critical |
   | ui.csp.domain_stripped               | CSP domains removed by mediation                       | Warning  |
   | ui.permission.denied                 | Permission removed by mediation                        | Warning  |
   | tool.invocation.app_driven           | Tool call originated from UI app                       | Info     |
   | tool.invocation.app_driven.blocked   | App-driven tool call blocked by policy                 | High     |
   | tool.invocation.app_driven.rate_limited | App-driven call exceeded rate limit                  | Warning  |

3. Each event type must include:
   - Standard fields: timestamp, event_type, agent (spiffe_id, session_id), trace_id
   - UI-specific fields: resource_uri, resource_content_hash, resource_size_bytes, content_type, hash_verified, scan_result, csp_mediation, permissions_mediation, capability_grant_mode
   - For tool.invocation.app_driven: ui_context (resource_uri, content_hash, originating_tool, session_id) and correlation (ui_session_start, tool_calls_in_ui_session, user_interaction_inferred)

4. Integration with existing audit middleware: The audit middleware (step 4 in the chain) already emits events. UI events are emitted from the response processing pipeline and from individual control functions. Ensure they follow the same structured JSON format, include hash_previous for hash chain integrity, and are routed to the same audit log sink.

FILES TO CREATE/MODIFY:
- internal/gateway/audit/ui_events.go (NEW): UI event type constants, UI audit event structs, emit functions
- internal/gateway/audit/audit.go: Register new event types, ensure UI events include hash chain
- Update all UI control functions (capability gating, resource controls, CSP mediation, tool-call mediation) to emit appropriate audit events using the new types

MANDATORY SKILLS TO REVIEW:
- None identified. Extends existing audit patterns established in the codebase.

Testing Requirements:
- Unit tests: Test UI event struct marshaling. Test each event type emits correct fields. Test severity assignment. Mocks acceptable for audit sink.
- Integration tests (MANDATORY, no mocks): Against running compose stack:
  - Trigger each of the 10 event types and verify they appear in audit log with correct structure
  - Verify hash chain integrity includes UI events (hash_previous links)
  - Verify trace_id correlation across related UI events in the same request
  - Verify event severity matches specification
acceptance_criteria:
1. All 10 UI event types implemented (ui.capability.stripped through tool.invocation.app_driven.rate_limited).
2. UI audit events follow the documented JSON schema (ui section with resource_uri, content_hash, scan_result, csp_mediation, permissions_mediation, etc.).
3. tool.invocation.app_driven events include ui_context and correlation subsections.
4. Severity levels correct: Info for reads/passthrough, Warning for stripped/denied, High for blocked, Critical for hash_mismatch.
5. UI events integrate with existing audit hash chain (hash_previous field).
6. UI events include trace_id for cross-event correlation.
7. Events routed to the same audit log sink as existing events.
8. Wiring verification - the UI event emit functions MUST be called from the UI control functions (capability gating, resource controls, CSP mediation, tool-call mediation). `grep -rn 'EmitUIEvent\|emitUIEvent\|ui\.capability\|ui\.resource\|ui\.csp\|ui\.permission' internal/gateway/` must show event emissions from both the event definitions AND from the control functions that call them.

- Hash chain: test output showing UI events include hash_previous field

- Commit SHA pushed to branch
